sourceDesc.p: Bibliography and direct anaylsis of the manuscipts

encodingDesc.p: persName, placeName and orgName can also be encoded as ref, so all references are in ref.
persname with @role="attribution" when what is recorded is the attribution

When constructing the value of a xml:id for msDesc, use settlement, underscore, abbreviation of the repository, underscore, shelfmark; use dashes only when necessary (for example to divide two sequences of numbers), but not to replace spaces.

msItem.title: Fragment of a commentary on DC
msItem.note: According to Lohr, the fragment of a commentary on DC at ff. 100ra-102ra is Versoris's text. However, neither the titles of the questions nor their content correspond to Versoris'commentary. The structure of these questions is indeed similar to that of Versoris’s commentary, with notabilia, conclusions and doubts.

msItem.title: Utrum corpus mobile ad locum sit subiectum scientie libri De celo et mundo
Utrum totum, omne et perfectum sint determinata tribus vicibus: principio, medio et fine
Utrum mundus sit magnitudo perfecta
msItem.note: Only the title was copied, but not the question
Expositio cum questionibus

msItem.title: Utrum scientia de anima sit pars philosophie naturalis
Utrum omnis notitia sit de numero bonorum honorabiliorum
Utrum scientia de anima sit de numero difficiliorum
msItem.note: Incomplete
Copied by a different hand than following text.

msItem.title: QdA
msItem.explicit: circa colores, et hoc est percipere plures The other leaves have been ripped off.
msItem.note: Copied by a different hand than previous text.
Unfinished, up to q. II Utrum sensus tactus sit unus vel plures

msItem.author: Lambertus de Monte
msItem.title: Copulata super tres libros De anima Aristotelis iuxta doctrinam Thomae Aquinatis
msItem.note: QdA is followed by the edition of Lambertus de Monte's Questions on the same text (Hain 11583)
Cologne editions of QdC, QsM, QdG and QPN printed by Molner in 1485.

origin.p: The watermark P is similar to Piccard XII 81 and 155, both used in Cologne in 1486. This dating and origin could be confirmed a) by the date of the editions inserted in the codex, all printed in 1485; b) by the fact that the ms. contains, above Versoris’s text, Lambertus de Monte’s Questions on De anima, a thomistic commentary used in Cologne.

note.p: QsP is particularly close to Basel F III 10.
The first commentary is very similar to the DA commentary transmitted in ms. Basel F I 13: the questions transmitted are the same; the structure is the same; it could be a copy of this text.

msItem.title: QsMete
msItem.note: The QsMete is interrupted at question II.1. They are followed by 19 blanck numbered leaves, after which the QdA begin.

origin.p: The watermark, a shield with three lilia couroned with flowers (particularly visible in the first guard leafs and at ff. 39, 87, 95, 116-117, 139, 188, 202, 204-205, 207, 256, 270, 295), is very similar to Briquet 1680, a variant of which was used in Paris in 1451. From 215-217, 221-222 variant with hook.

provenance.p: Just like ms. Amiens 405, it belonged to monastery of St. Pierre in Corby. On f. 1r, in the centre of the decoration, a later (17th century) hand wrote: monasterii sancti Petri Corbeiensis.

listBibl.bibl: Catalogue général des manuscrits des bibliothèques publiques de France. Départements – tome XIX : Amiens, 191-192.

msItem.title: QsPol
msItem.note: The text is preceded by a prologue, inc.: Cum omnium artium que ad rectam vivendi viam perducunt ratio et discipline sapientie studio quam philosophiam appellant contineatur.

origin.p: The manuscript can be tentatively dated and localized on the basis of a fleur-de-lis watermark corresponding to Briquet 7216, attested in Angers in 1467.

msItem.title: QsE I–X
msItem.colophon: Expliciunt questiones super primo Ethicorum, 1471

origin.p: Dating stated in the colophon of the first book: Expliciunt questiones super primo Ethicorum, 1471, f. 14r.

The first part of this manuscript, containing Versoris's commentary, was copied in Basel. The manuscript was bound in Basel together with an incunable printed in 1475, as attested by the binder's stamp of Pancratius Hochberg of Basel.

provenance.p: The codex belonged to Ambrosius Allantsee, with whom it arrived in 1491 at the Benedictine convent of St. Mang in Füssen. On the inner front cover, there is an ex libris: Sancti Magni in Fuessen. A later ex libris indicates that the manuscript subsequently came into the possession of the library of the Church of Saints Ulrich and Afra in Augsburg.

listBibl.bibl: H. Hilg, Lateinische mittelalterliche Handschriften in Folio der Universitätsbibliothek Augsburg: Cod. II. 1.2o 91–226, Wiesbaden, 1999, p. 375–377

msItem.title: QsE
msItem.note: The text ends unfinished at book IX, question Utrum amare seipsum secundum rationem sit laudabile, with the words: Nam loco bonorum exteriorum eligit bonum virtutis, quod est maximum, et sic.

origin.p: This dating appears in the colophon of Aristotle’s Nicomachean Ethics (2r-150r): Anno Domini 1493, f. 150r.

The codex is the fourth and final part of a collection containing Aristotle’s corpus, with the first three volumes transmitting the Metaphysics, logic, and natural philosophy. The four volumes were copied between the years 1492 and 1493.

provenance.p: The manuscript belonged to Bamberg Chapter library

summary.ab: The commentary is ascribed to Versoris in the first colophon

msItem.title: QsDente
msItem.note: The commentary is preceded by Thomas’s text (88v-106r). The manuscript also contains a literal commentary on Thomas’s text (137r-170r).
msItem.colophon: Explicit scriptum Versoris super De ente et essentia sancti Thome doctoris

msItem.title: Thomas's Text Commentary
msItem.incipit: insignis perypatetice veritatis interpres Doctor sanctus nedum adultos provehi, verum in novitios afficiens erudire uberiores
msItem.explicit: ipsa lux non eclipsalis, sed lucens eternaliter

origin.p: In the colophon of f. 35r (Henricus Collis’s Sermones contra articulos plebanorum Argentinensium), we read that the text was copied in 1456: Scriptum per me, fratrem Nicolaum Unbehuen de Bamberga in Ulma, tunc temporis magister studentium, anno Domini 1456 in quadragesima. The date 1465 likely appears in the second colophon of Versoris’s commentary, though it is difficult to confirm because the colophon has been deleted. However, I could distinguish the first words: Explicit De ente et essentia magistri Versoris.
The manuscript was copied in Ulm by Nicolaus Unbehuen (as stated on f. 35r) or Umbehauen (as stated on f. 137r).

provenance.p: It belonged to the Dominican convent, as indicated on f. 1r: ordinis predicatorum.

listBibl.bibl: S. Włodek, “Thomas Sutton, De productione formae substantialis”, AHDLMA 46 (1979), 127- 175, at 129.

msItem.locus: Paper guard-leaf at the beginning of the codex
msItem.quote: Liber Cartusiensis in Basilea minori preveniens a domine Iohanne de Lapide confratre nostro, et continentur in eo: Questiones super sex primos libros Metaphysice Aristotelis; Questiones super sex primos libros Ethicorum Aristotelis; libri Ethicorum Aristotelis; libpri Yconomicorum eiusdem [not transmitted]; liber de doctrina tradendi et loquendi Albertani [not transmitted]

msItem.note: I-VI: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto primo libro Metaphysice, finito et finato(?) per me Iacobum Vngelink de Stendal, clericum dyocesis Halbertstadensis, Parisius in collegio Tornacensi, die ultima mensis Octobris, vel in vigilia omnium sanctorum, anno Domini M° CCCC° LXVJ° hora quasi undecima in meridie

[Book II of the Ethics] anno Domini M° CCCC° LXVJ°, die nona mensis septembris, in collegio Tornacensi Parisius

origin.p: The texts were copied by Iacobus Ungelink de Stendal from Halberstadt, student at the Collège of Tournai in Paris.

note.p: Basel, UB, F III 10; F III 11; F III 12; F VI 16; F VI 67; F VII 11, which were also owned by Iohannes de Lapide.
Basel, UB, F III 10; F VI 67;F VII 11, which were also copied by Iohannes de Lapide.

provenance.p: The codex was acquired by Iohannes de Lapide, who brought it to the Carthusian Convent in Basel.

msItem.quote: Contenta in hoc libro: Questiones magistri Versoris super octo libros Phisicorum; Idem super duos De celo et mundo primos et super quartum; tertius vero desinit; Idem super libros De generatione et corruptione; idem super libros Metheororum; idem super libros De sensu et sensato; idem super librum De memoria et reminiscentia; idem super librum De somno et vigilia; idem super tres libros De anima; idem super librum De longitudine et brevitate vite; idem super omnes libros Methaphysice.

msItem.title: QdC
msItem.note: Ascription to Versoris can be found at the end of the first book of the Questions on De celo, f. 131ra. The colophon by the hand of Iohannes Scolaris reads: Et sic est finis primi De celi et mundi. Another hand added: Iohannis Versoris.
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis secundi De celo et mundo, in vigilia phipi Iacob anno Domini LXVIII°

origin.p: The manuscript was copied by Iohannes Scholaris in 1458-59, just like ms. F III 11.

In the first part of the volume, the same watermark that can also be found in ms. F III 11, is quite similar (except for the lower part) to Piccard, Lilie, III 1453 (Metz, 1477); in the second part of the volume, a mermaid very similar to Briquet 13858 (Troyes, 1458-1461).

note.p: Basel, UB, F II 14; F III 11; F III 12; F VI 16; F VI 67; F VII 11, which were also owned by Iohannes de Lapide.

summary.ab: The texts are ascribed to Versoris in the table of contents written by a medieval hand at the beginning of the volume

msItem.incipit: Questiones magistri Versoris super Veterem Artem; Idem, super librum Sex principiorum Gilberti Porretani; Idem super libros Prioru et Posteriorum; Idem super quattuor libros Topicorum primos; Idem super libros Elencorum; Idem super librum divisionum Boetii; Idem super librum beati Thome doctoris eximii De esse et essentia.

origin.p: In the colophon of De sex principiis we read the initials of Iohannes scolaris (I S), who copied in 1460 another codex containing Versoris's works, ms. F III 10.
The watermark is quite similar (except for the lower part) to Piccard, Lilie, III 1453 (Metz, 1477)

note.p: Basel, UB, F II 14; F III 10; F III 12; F VI 16; F VI 67; F VII 11, which were also owned by Iohannes de Lapide.
Basel, UB, F III 10; F VI 67;F VII 11, which were also copied by Iohannes de Lapide.

provenance.p: Belonged to the Basel Dominican Convent

origin.origDate: 1460-1461, with a note by Erber added in 1464.
origin.p: The manuscript originated at Paris University. The treatise de intentionibus secundis (ff. 2r–6r) was copied by Georgius Erber, at that time student at the Collège de la Marche, in 1461: Scriptum Parisius in collegio Magne Marchie, anno Domini 1461, in die Trinitatis, finitum per manus Georgii Erber, ibidem tunc tempore studentem, f. 6r. The commentaries on the logical corpus ascribed to Versoris were copied between 1460 and 1461 at the Collège de Bourgogne by Rudolfus de Lo: Et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto libro Priorum, finite per manus Rudolfi de Lo, alumni altissimi reverendique collegii Burgundiae, anno Domini M CCCC LXVIII in vigilia Iudica, f. 125rb; Et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto Posteriorum Analeticorum Aristotelis. Laus Deo, 1460, f. 168rb; Et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto libro Topicorum Aristotelis sufficiant. Pro quo laudetur qui omnibus esse habetur. Finita Parisius anno Domini 1461 incipiente, f. 202ra. Georgius Erber bought the manuscript in the same year: Anno Domini 1461 finita est hec loyca Arestotelis, comparata per Georgius Erber de Aybling, protunc Parisius studentem, f. 229rb. Later on, in 1464, Georgius Erber, by then Master of Arts in Coburg, asked a certain Matheus Gutgegen de Mapurck, who previously was a rabbi called Beniamin, to copy the Hebraic alphabet: Ego, Georgius Erber, in artibus magister, pro tunc scolasticus in Coburgk, hoc alphabetum rogavi scribere quendam nomine Matheum Gutgegen de Mapurck olim existentem iudeum, nomine Beniamin cum uxore Maria quorum filius Abraham et filia Rachel, morientes pro tunc in Montua [Mantua?] anno domini 1464 in die sancte Trinitatis. Ego autem prenominatus hoc alphabetum litteris latinis sic signavi eo presente et me in hoc docente, qui et olim rabbi iudeorum fuit, f. 243v.

note.p: Paris, BnF, latin 6691; Wrocław, BU, Ms. IV F 1, which were also copied in the Collège de Bourgogne.
Berlin, SB – PK, lat. fol. 402, which also belonged to Georgius Erber.

provenance.p: The manuscript later came to Erfurt; on the inner front cover we read: Liber sancti Petri in Erfordia.

history.acquisition: It entered the possession of the Berlin State Library in 1995, when it was purchased at an auction at Gerda Bassenge.

listBibl.bibl: C. Meyer, “La compilation sur la musique de Georgius Erber (Paris, 1460-1462). Innsbruck, Universitatsbibliothek, Cod. 962, f. 128r-163r” [introduction, commentary and text edition (uploaded in HAL 18 mai 2017): https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-01522251, 2, n. 4-5.
K. Heydeck, Die mittelalterlichen Handschriften der Universitätsbibliothek Rostock, Wiesbaden, 2001, 33.

msItem.title: Summule logicales
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis textus Summularum magistri Petri Hispani ordinis Predicatorum, qui quidem completus est Parisius in collegio Tornacensi, anno incarnationis Domini 1463, per me, Christianum Lupi, magno cum labore

msItem.title: CsPH
msItem.note: Versoris’s commentary is preceded by the text of the Summule logicales (ff. 1r–63r).
msItem.colophon: finitius est liber Parisius, anno Domini incarnationis 1463, per me, Christianum Lupi

origin.p: Paris, as stated in the colophons: Et sic est finis textus Summularum magistri Petri Hispani ordinis Predicatorum, qui quidem completus est Parisius in collegio Tornacensi, anno incarnationis Domini 1463, per me, Christianum Lupi, magno cum labore, f. 63r; finitius est liber Parisius, anno Domini incarnationis 1463, per me, Christianum Lupi, f. 292v. Christianus Lupi later entered the franciscain convent in Brandeburg, which explains the presence of this codex in Berlin.

provenance.p: On f. 294v, we find the following ex libris: Iste liber spectat ad conventum Branderbugensis.

listBibl.bibl: V. Rose, Verzeichniss der Lateinischen Handschriften der Königlichen Bibliothek zu Berlin. Zweiter Band: Die Handschriften der Kurfürstlichen Bibliothek und der Kurfürstlichen Lande. Dritte Abteilung, Berlin, 1905, 1246, n. 981
Meirinhos, Bibliotheca manuscripta Petri Hispani, 61 ascribing the commentary to Versoris with doubts

msItem.title: QsP
msItem.quote: secundo nonas febriarii. Da waß Dietherus aller morchin frij
Deo gratias, per manus Dietheri de Darmstad scriptum perhenniter prima die Martii suo cognoomine Billynger
Hunc de Wormacia / plena gracia / conserva Maria. Septima ydus Martii
Per me fratrem Dietherum Billinger de Darmstad filium conventus Wormaciensis in Colonia, tertie idus Martii
msItem.colophon: Et in hac finitur summa octavi Phisicorum … Johannis Versoris Parisiensis … Ego frater Dietherus Billinger de Darmstad alias dictus de Wormacia scripsi et cum magno labore finivi Colonie in nostro convent subditusque studii … anno domini MCCCC sexagesimo, finis octavo datur Apprilis secundo:

msItem.title: QdLong
msItem.colophon: per manus fratris Werhneri de Hirczhorn anno domini 1472 in die cene infra vesperas

msItem.title: QsMetaph
msItem.colophon: Et in hoc finitur tabula super XII libros Metaphysice extracta de questionibus Versoris per manus fratris Werneri de Hircrzhorn, ordinis carmelitarum anno Domini 1473, et fui tunc temporis magister studentium conventus Treverensis, licet immeritus

origin.p: The texts in this manuscript have been copied in Cologne and Trier by three scribes: Werner Wacker de Welstatt de Hirschhorn, Carmelite, student in Cologne from 1464 to 1472 and from 1474 to 1476, lector in Trier from 1473 to 1474 (Lickteig, German Carmelites, p. 454, 484); Theodericus Billinger de Darmstadt, Carmelite, studied in Cologne in 1463-1464 and 1467-1468 (Lickteig, German Carmelites, p. 453); Johannes Dinck.

listBibl.bibl: Ch. Lohr, “Aristotelica Germanica I: Bonn, Darmstadt, Koblenz, Paderborn”, in Studia mediewistyczne 34/35 (2000) p. 289–299, at p. 289–290
J. Geiß, Katalog der mittelalterlichen Handschriften der Universitäts- und Landesbibliothek Bonn, Berlin, 2015, p. 2–5.

summary.ab: No. All the texts in this codex are transmitted anonymously and copied by different hands.

msItem.quote: De libris † … † ordinis predicatorum † … † 1491. Presens liber pertinet conventui Gewilerensis ordinis fratrum predicatorum.

msItem.title: QsP
msItem.colophon: scriptus per me Eberhardum Saltzmann de valle masonis, anno M° CCCC° quinquagesimo nono, feria quarta ante Simonis et Iude festum beatorum apostolorum

msItem.title: QdA
msItem.note: Anonymous QdA. The first folio, probably containing an eluminure, has been ripped out. The collation of the QdA and the QPN with Johannes de Magistris’s commentaries enabled to identify the anonymous texts in the Colmar manuscript with them.
msItem.incipit: animam esse nobiliorem aut honorabiliorem. Altera autem, quia est certior …

msItem.title: QSom
msItem.note: Should be attribute to another Parisian master, John of Caulaincourt.

origin.p: The texts were copied by Eberhardum Saltzmann de valle masonis, Masevaux, diocese of Basel.

1459, as stated in the colophon of QsP. Although, at the end of book I (f. 12v), we read (by a different hand) the date 1491, the previous dating is confirmed by the watermak: a P similar to Briquet 8593 (widespread, among other places, in Basel, 1461-1464, and in Strasbourg, 1463).

the volume consists of three parts (Metaph; Phy; QdA, PN) copied by different hands and presenting different watermarks. The QsM/QsP and the QdA/QPN are separated by a guard leaf. The QdA/QPN contain a watermark similar to Piccard Lilie I 683-686 (Frankfurt a.M., Aachen Cologne, Brugge, 1478-1481). Their origin seems therefore to be different from the parts containing the QsM and the QsP.
Many of the enluminures at the beginning of the books have been ripped out.
origin.note: The section transmitting the QsP contains the same watermark (Frucht similar to Piccard I 393-398) of ms. Basel, UB F VI 4

provenance.p: The manuscript belonged to the convent of the Dominicans of Guebwiller.

listBibl.bibl: Bibliography: Catalogue général des manuscrits des bibliothèques publiques de France. Départements. Tome 56. Bibliothèque de Colmar, Paris 1969, pp. 144-145

summary.ab: The text is ascribed to John Versoris in the colophon of the second owner and corrector.

msItem.title: QsP
msItem.colophon: Finis feliciter octavi Phisicorum anno Domini M CCCC LXXIIII°, per me, Iohannem Hecht de Memingen, studentem alme universitatis Haydelbergensis

origin.p: the text was copied in 1474 and corrected in 1478.

Versoris’s text was copied by the student Iohannes Hecht from Memmingen and later corrected by the new owner of the manuscript, the master Henricus Stitz from Butzbach, as is evident from the colophons: QsP, f. 90ra. A few lines by a different hand have been crossed out in the following column, but the text remains partly legible: Per me, artium liberalium magistrum, magistrum Henricum Stitz ex Butzbach Heidelbergensem, empte sunt hec questiones, notanter per me, magistrum Henricum Stizt, emendate et correcte. Questiones Versoris super VIII libros Phisicorum Aristotelis, per florenis in auro sex, in studio meo … Heidelbergensi in anno Domini 1478. The same hand added two lines that were not erased: Correcte sunt hee questiones Versoris super VIII libros Phisicorum Aristotelis, perfecte in anno 1478, f. 90rb.
On f. 1r, we read: Henricus Musinger est possessor huius libri.

listBibl.bibl: W. G. Bayerer, Die Handschriften des ehem. Fraterherrenstifts St. Markus zu Butzbach, Teil 1: Hss. aus der Nummernfolge Hs 42–Hs 760 (Hs.kat. UB Gießen 4,1), Wiesbaden 1980, p. 3-5.

ab.note: The manuscript also transmits a set of Cologne questions on Thomas Aquinas’s De ente et essentia (ff. 293r–299v; colophon: “Et sic est finis questionum super tractatu sancti Thome de quiditatibus entium in universitate Coloniensi collectarum”) and a set of Cologne questions on Thomas’s De natura generis (ff. 306v–316v).

Interestingly, this manuscript contains three commentaries on Aristotle’s Metaphysics: two in the tradition of the via moderna and one in the tradition of the via antiqua: John Buridan’s ultima lectura (ff. 14r–147r); Nicolaus of Amsterdam’s (ff. 148r–227r); and the compilation consisting of Versoris’s commentary and the Cologne commentary.

msItem.author: John Buridan
msItem.title: Ultima lectura. Commentaries on Aristotle’s Metaphysics
msItem.colophon: Finitum per Petrum Juterbog, <sub>/<pro> reverendo magistro de Collebergh, [ scrips. sed del.: in vigilia] sabbato post festum sancte Katherine, anno Domini 1446 in studio Lipczensi

msItem.author: Versoris
msItem.title: QsMeta VII–X and XII
msItem.note: QsMeta VII–X and XII (ff. 263r–292v) correspond to the text ascribed to Versoris in part of the manuscript tradition as well as in the Cologne editions. Books I–II, IV–VI (ff. 233r–263r) differ from Versoris’s text.

msItem.title: De ente et essentia
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis questionum super tractatu sancti Thome de quiditatibus entium in universitate Coloniensi collectarum

origin.p: The manuscript was copied in its entirety by the Leipzig student Petrus Juterbog, writing for (or under the supervision of) the master Theodericus Steffani de Colberg, as stated in the colophon on f. 12vb. Theodericus Steffani moved to the University of Greifswald in 1457, bringing the codex with him.

note.p: Gießen, Universitätsbibliothek, Hs. 618; Leipzig, Universitätsbibliothek, Ms 1384; Leipzig, Universitätsbibliothek, Ms 1419; Zeitz, Domherrenbibliothek, LIX (24), which contain the same Cologne questions on Aristotle’s Metaphysics.

provenance.p: The manuscript later came into the possession of the Dominican convent in Greifswald and, after its suppression, passed to the Stadtpfarrkirche St. Marien; since 1602 it has belonged to the Geistliches Ministerium.

listBibl.bibl: J. Geiss, Mittelalterliche Handschriften in Greifswalder Bibliotheken, Wiesbaden 2009, p. 59-61.

msItem.author: Lambertus de Monte
msItem.title: Copulata ... super 8 libros phisicorum Aristotelis
msItem.note: Cologne, Heinrich Quentell, 1493

msItem.author: Lambertus de Monte
msItem.title: Copulata super libros de anima Aristotelis
msItem.note: Cologne, Heinrich Quentell, 1494

msItem.author: Lambertus de Monte
msItem.title: Positiones circa libros phisicorum et de anima Aristotelis
msItem.note: Cologne, Heinrich Quentell, 1494

origin.p: Shortly before 1495. This dating can be inferred from the watermarks, the note on the inner back cover, and the dates of the printed works bound in the volume, namely Lambertus de Monte’s Copulata on Aristotle’s Physics (Cologne, Heinrich Quentell, 1493; Hain 1686, GW M16784) and De anima (Cologne, Heinrich Quentell, 1494; Hain 1713, GW M16775), as well as the Positiones circa libros Phisicorum et De anima Aristotelis (Cologne, Heinrich Quentell, 1494; Hain 13303, GW M35127).

The manuscript was owned by Petrus Hunlin de Lindaw, a student in Heidelberg, who purchased it in 1495. On the inner back cover we read: Iste liber est beate virginis Marie in Salem, comparatus per fratrem Petrum Hienlin, ibidem professum pro 1 ½ florenis et VII albis cum ligatura 1495to.

listBibl.bibl: W. Wilfried, Die mittelalterlichen nichtliturgischen Handschriften des Zisterzienserklosters Salem, Wiesbaden 2000, p. 156–157.

summary.ab: The commentaries are ascribed to Versoris in the note on the inner front cover and in the table of contents on the first guard leaf: in isto libro continentur primo questiones magistri Versoris super Porphirium, item Predicamente Aristotelis….

origin.p: After the suppression of the monastery in 1808, the codex arrived at the Tirol University Library. On the inner front cover we read: Dicta Versoris super totam loycam. Liber virginis gloriose in Stams, comparatus per me, fratrem Melchior de Ymbst, per iii florenos, in alma universitate Heidelbergensi, anno Domini MCCCCLXXII, tunc temporis occupantem studium. [a.m.:] Frater Balthazar de Ymbst, venerabilis fratris Melchior confrater carnalis necnon spiritualis, qui docti fuerunt et ad iustitiam erudiverunt multos, fulgebunt quasi stelle in perpetuas eternitates. This information also appears in other places of the codex, such as in the first guard leaf: frater Melchior de Stans, anno Domini etc. LXXII occupavit studium alme universitatis Haidelbergensis, and on ff. 264v: Stams me possidet liber dicit iste. Melchior de Stams me comparavit. Melchior me emit. On the back guard-leaves students at Heidelberg University in 1472 and 1488 are listed (Anno Domini MCCCCLXXII studentes apud sanctum Iacobum extra muros alme universitatis Heidelbergensis; Anno nostre salutis autore millesimo CCCCmo 88vo fuere studentes apud sanctum Iacobum extra muros Haydelberge; the list of the students can be consulted in the modern catalogue description).
origin.note: and present similar watermarks, possibly indicating a common origin.

provenance.p: The codex belonged to Melchior Bosch from Imst, who studied at Heidelberg University and later entered the Cistercensian monastery of Stams. It later came into the possession of Balthasar Bosch, from the same family as Melchior, who also studied at Heidelberg University and entered the Cistercensian monastery of Stams.

listBibl.bibl: K. Walsh, “Zwei Stamser Zisterzienser an der Universität Heidelberg: Melchior und Balthasar Bosch aus Imst als Besitzer von Innsbruck, Universitätsbibliothek Cod. 220, und ihr Freundeskreis”, in Kulturerbe und Bibliotheksmanagement. Festschrift für Walter Neuhauser (Biblos-Schriften 170), Innsbruck 1998, 89–122

G. Kompatscher, Katalog der Handschriften der Universitätsbibliothek Innsbruck, Teil 3: Cod. 201–300. Unter Mitarbeit von Walter Neuhauser, Sieglinde Sepp, Eva Ramminger. Katalog- und Registerband, Wien 1999, 71–78, Abb. 4 (1r), Abb. 30, 74–75

C. Schretter-Picker, “Libri Stamsenses. Anfang und Werden der mittelalterlichen Büchersammlung des Zisterzienserstiftes Stams”, in J. Hörmann-Thurn und Taxis, T. Pamer, J. Schwarz (edd.), Anfang und Werden –– Stift Stams im Mittelalter. Vorträge der wissenschaftlichen Tagung anlässlich des 750-Jahr-Jubiläums des Zisterzienserstiftes Stams 1273–2023. Stift Stams, 22.–24. September 2024 (Schlern-Schriften 376), Innsbruck 2023, 273–29, at 288

msItem.author: Joannes Versoris
msItem.title: Questions on the Ars nova; De ente et essentia
msItem.note: Printed in Cologne by Heinrich Quentell before 1489

msItem.author: Joannes Versoris
msItem.title: Questions on the Ars vetus (Isagoge, Predicamenta, De interpretatione, De sex principiis)
msItem.note: Printed in Cologne around 1487

msItem.title: QsPor
msItem.note: These questions are preceded by two incunabula: 1. Versoris’s Questions on the Ars nova and De ente et essentia; 2. Versoris’s Questions on the Ars vetus (Isagoge, Predicamenta, De interpretatione, De sex principiis) and followed by two anonymous manuscript copies of commentaries on the Ars vetus: a set of Questions on Porphyry’s Isagoge

msItem.title: Questions on Porphyry’s Isagoge
msItem.incipit: Circa initium Porphyrii queritur primo utrum logica rationalis scientia specialiter ab aliis scientiis sit distincta. Pro cuius questionis determinatione tria sunt videnda
msItem.explicit: penes modum essendi et predicandi de multis distinguuntur

msItem.title: Questions on Aristotle’s Predicamenta
msItem.incipit: Circa initium libri Predicamentorum Aristotelis queritur primo utrum de predicamentis sit scientia. Pro quod sciendum primo quod, quia scientia est habitus intellectualis, videndum est quid sit habitus, quot sunt et quomodo distinguuntur
msItem.explicit: supplet vicem speciei intelligibilis; est tamen sciendum…

origin.origDate: End of 15th.Century
origin.p: The dating of the incunabula bound together in the codex (around 1487–89; see the section on content) and the list of lectures attended by Iacobus from 1497 to 1500 (f. 204r) suggest that the volume was assembled at the end of the fifteenth century.

provenance.p: The codex belonged to Iacobus Borkeman de Mariaeburgo, as indicated by the possession mark on the upper margin of f. 2r: Iacobi Borkeman Mariaenburgensis sum.

msItem.title: QsSE
msItem.note: The manuscript also contains Laurentius de Poznania’s literal commentaries on Aristotle’s Prior Analytics (ff. 1r–75r), Posterior Analytics (ff. 79r–135r), and De anima (ff. 148r–158r), as well as a fragment from his literal commentary on the Sophistical Refutations (f. 135v). In addition, it includes a copy of Aristotle’s Sophistical Refutations with some anonymous marginal and interlinear glosses (ff. 198r–252v), and an anonymous literal commentary on Aristotle’s Prior Analytics (ff. 136r–146v).
msItem.colophon: Anno Domini millesimo quadrigentesimo septuagesimo septimo

origin.p: 1477, as evident from the colophon: Anno Domini millesimo quadrigentesimo septuagesimo septimo, f. 286v.

msItem.title: Circa tertium De celo et mundo queritur utrum ex parte gravitatis et levitatis possit concludi nullum corpus esse compositum ex indivisibilibus
Utrum ex parte gravitatis et levitatis numerus quaternarius sit elementorum
Utrum elementa secundum se tota sint generabilia et corruptibilia

note.p: Versoris’s QdC I–II, IV are completed by some questions on Book III, copied without codicological discontinuity between Book II and Book IV of Versoris’s commentary. The questions are as follows: Circa tertium De celo et mundo queritur utrum ex parte gravitatis et levitatis possit concludi nullum corpus esse compositum ex indivisibilibus, f. 126rb; Utrum omnium corporum sit generatio, f. 127r; Utrum ex parte gravitatis et levitatis numerus quaternarius sit elementorum, f. 128r; Utrum elementa secundum se tota sint generabilia et corruptibilia, f. 128v. The same questions have been interpolated into Versoris’s QdC in ms. BJ 2024.
The QsMete are preceded by the prologue of Paulus of Worczyn’s Questions on Aristotle’s Meteorology.

To complete the set of commentaries on Aristotle’s libri naturales, the codex also contains the anonymous Cracovian QsP (inc.: circa initium libri Physicorum queritur utrum inter tres scientias speculativas reales philosophia ordine doctrine sit prima, mathematica secunda, metaphysica tertia”) and some anonymous QdG (ff. 135r–163r, the same that can be found in ms. 2118, ff. 177r–193r).
The commentaries are accompanied by several glosses.

msItem.author: Anonymus
msItem.title: QsP
msItem.incipit: circa initium libri Physicorum queritur utrum inter tres scientias speculativas reales philosophia ordine doctrine sit prima, mathematica secunda, metaphysica tertia

origin.p: Based on the watermarks, the part containing Versoris’s commentaries can be dated to the third quarter of the fifteenth century. I am referring to the typescript description prepared by Marian Zwiercan in 1961, which was kindly made available to me by the librarians of the manuscript department.

note.p: Kraków, BJ, cod. 2085, which also belonged to Michael de Turobin.
Kraków, BJ, cod. 2024, in which Versoris's QdC are also completed with the same anonymous questions on Book III.
Krakow, BJ, cod. 2002, 2072, 2087, which contain the same Cracovian QsP.
Kraków, BJ, cod. 2118, which contains the same anonymous QdG found at ff. 135r–163r.
Munich, BSB, Clm 520, in which Versoris's QsMete are also preceded by the prologue from Paulus of Worczyn's Questions on Aristotle's Meteorology.

provenance.p: The codex belonged to Michael de Turobin: Liber Michaelis Lublin, alias de Turobin, decretorum doctoris, decani Cureloviensis, canonici Lovicensis et fundatoris ecclesie Collegialis ad sanctam Annam Cracovie, datus in usum pauperum studiosorum, qui obiit 1528, 24 Aprilis, f. 1r.

listBibl.bibl: Panzica, “Commenter les Météorologiques à l’Université de Cracovie”, 122–124, 125, 178.

msItem.author: Johannes Versoris
msItem.title: Qdente
msItem.note: Versoris’s questions are followed by some anonymous QdC (ff. 25r–40v); QdG (ff. 40v–53r); QsE (ff. 53r–107r); Puncta on the Metaphysics (ff. 110v–171v); QsTop (ff. 175r–216r).

origin.p: 1460. This date appears in the colophon of some anonymous Puncta on Books I–XII of Aristotle’s Metaphysics, as well as on the first guard leaf, both by the same scribe.

Probably Krakow. Part of this manuscript was copied by Baltasar from Poznań in 1460: Expliciunt puncta Metaphysice, per me, Baltasar de Poznania, finita feria tertia proxima ante festum sancti Gregori, sub anno Domini M°CCCC° sexagesimo, f. 171v. The name of this scribe and the date also appear on the first folio: Pisana przez Baltara z Poznania, 1460 roku.

provenance.p: The manuscript belonged to a master named Kazuchowski, as we read at f. 1r: Mathie, liber magistri artium Kazuchowski.

summary.ab: The table of contents at f. 1r ascribes the first set of Questions on De anima to Versoris, and describes the Questions on the Parva naturalia as written according to the via antiqua: Liber magistri Andree de Gąbyn. Questiones Versoris super libros De anima; Questiones alie breves super libros De anima; Questiones super Parva naturalia de via antiquorum; Liber de differentia spiritus et anime; Problemata. The ascription to Versoris of the first set of questions is found again at f. 2r (Questiones Versoris super De anima), but neither the incipit, nor the explicit, nor the list of questions corresponds to Versoris’s commentary.

msItem.title: QsSens
msItem.note: These commentaries are preceded by two anonymous sets of questions on Aristotle’s De anima (ff. 2r–81v and ff. 82r–111r). A set of Problemata (ff. 188r–218r) completes the volume.
Versoris’s commentaries are accompanied by quite dense marginal glosses.

msItem.title: QdLong
msItem.colophon: anno Domini 1473

origin.p: Versoris’s commentaries were copied in 1473, as evident from the colophon (anno Domini 1473, f. 150v). The Problemata at the end of the volume were copied in 1467: Et sic est finis huius. Laudatur etiam Sanctus Bartholomeus, anno Domini 1467, per me, baccalarium de Falkenberg, f. 218r.

A bachelor from Falkenberg signed the colophon of the last text. The exact identification of this place name is uncertain, as several towns called existed in late medieval Germany (e.g., in Brandenburg, Saxony, and Bavaria). Given the strong presence of German students from these regions at the University of Kraków during the 15th century, the reference may plausibly point to one of the Saxon or Brandenburg Falkenbergs, though a precise identification cannot be established.

provenance.p: The codex was partly copied by and belonged to the master Andrea de Gąbyn, as stated in the colophon at f. 184v (per Andream de Gambyn, ipso die, etiam ante Penthecostem, anno Domini 1473) and in the possession mark at f. 1r.

msItem.title: QsE I–X
msItem.note: The commentary is accompanied by marginal notes in hands different from that of the main text.
msItem.colophon: Finitus anno Domini 1469, per fratrem Johannem comparatus

origin.p: The colophon records that the copy was completed in 1469: Finitus anno Domini 1469, per fratrem Johannem comparatus (f. 186r).

provenance.p: The codex (or sam of ist parts) belonged to the Krakow masters Andreas de Paradiso (magistri Andree de Paradiso, f. 193v, marg. sup.), Petrus Zambrzecz (first inside cover: tres sexterniones aput magistrum Petrum Zambrzecz), and Stanislaus Białkowski (first guard leaf: magistri Stanislai Bialkowski).

msItem.title: Circa tertium De celo et mundo queritur utrum ex parte gravitatis et levitatis possit concludi nullum corpus esse compositum ex indivisibilibus
Utrum ex parte gravitatis et levitatis numerus quaternarius sit elementorum
Utrum elementa secundum se tota sint generabilia et corruptibilia

note.p: Versoris’s QdC I–II, IV are completed by some questions on Book III, copied without codicological discontinuity between Book II and Book IV of his commentary. The questions are as follows: Circa tertium De celo et mundo queritur utrum ex parte gravitatis et levitatis possit concludi nullum corpus esse compositum ex indivisibilibus, f. 333r; Utrum omnium corporum sit generatio, f. 334r; Utrum ex parte gravitatis et levitatis numerus quaternarius sit elementorum, f. 334v; Utrum elementa secundum se tota sint generabilia et corruptibilia, f. 335r. The same questions have been interpolated into Versoris’s QdC in ms. Kraków, BJ, cod. 2007.

To complete the set of commentaries on Aristotle’s libri naturales, Versoris’s texts are preceded by John Breslawer de Brunsbgerga’s QsP (ff. 1r–259v) and followed by Andreas Ruczel de Costen (Kościan)’s QdA (ff. 400r–439r).

msItem.title: QdG
msItem.colophon: Explicit liber secundus De generatione et corruptione sub anno Domini 1478, in die Mathie ante reminiscere, feria quarta, etc.

origin.p: 1478, as stated in the colophon of the QdG: Explicit liber secundus De generatione et corruptione sub anno Domini 1478, in die Mathie ante reminiscere, feria quarta, etc., f. 294r.

note.p: Kraków, BJ, cod. 2007, in which Versoris's QdC are also completed with the same anonymous questions on Book III.

provenance.p: A mark of possession on the inner front cover indicates that the codex belonged to the Kraków master Clemens de Gambycze (Apud executores testamenti olim venerabilis viri maystri Clementis de Gambycze per eundem).

listBibl.bibl: Panzica, “Commenter les Météorologiques à l’Université de Cracovie”, 122–124, 126, 129, 130, 149, 178–179.

msItem.author: Antonius Andreae
msItem.title: QsMeta
msItem.colophon: Expliciunt questiones Anthonii Andree, ordinis minorum, correpte ac emendate per excellentissimum magistrum Franciscum de Nerithono, ordinis predicatorum, reportate in studio Cracoviensi per Martinum de Tarnoviecz, finite die Saturni immediate ante Galli in Maiori collegio, anno reparationis nostre 1489

msItem.title: Qdente
msItem.note: The text is preceded by Antonius Andreae’s QsMeta (ff. 4r–103r), followed by anonymous QsMeta (ff. 110r–185v) with marginal glosses, and then by Giles of Rome’s Questions on De ente et essentia (ff. 221v–243v).
msItem.colophon: terminate sunt hee questiones, propositiones Egidii Romani sub annis Domini tunc currentibus 1479

msItem.author: Giles of Rome
msItem.title: Questions on De ente et essentia
msItem.colophon: Martinus de Tarnoviecz scripsit, clericus Cracovie, infra caniculam

origin.p: The texts in this manuscript were copied between 1479 and 1489, as indicated by the colophons: Expliciunt questiones Anthonii Andree, ordinis minorum, correpte ac emendate per excellentissimum magistrum Franciscum de Nerithono, ordinis predicatorum, reportate in studio Cracoviensi per Martinum de Tarnoviecz, finite die Saturni immediate ante Galli in Maiori collegio, anno reparationis nostre 1489 (f. 103r); 1480 (f. 199r); terminate sunt hee questiones, propositiones Egidii Romani sub annis Domini tunc currentibus 1479 (f. 243r).

Versoris’s text was copied in Cracow by Martinus de Tarnowiecz, as stated in the colophon: Martinus de Tarnoviecz scripsit, clericus Cracovie, infra caniculam (f. 221r). Martinus later owned the volume, as attested by a note on f. 1r, written when he had already attained the title of master: ego magister Martinus, 1501.

msItem.title: QdLon
msItem.note: only the beginning of q. 1; ends interrupted after the oppositum, with the words: Pro responsione est primo sciendum quod

origin.p: The watermarks were attested in the years 1447–1457 I am referring to the typescript description prepared by Marian Zwiercan in 1961, which was kindly made available to me by the librarians of the manuscript department.

provenance.p: The manuscript belonged to Albert de Szurkowo, who studied in Paris and donated the codex to his heirs (f. 67rb: Liber reverendi magistri Alberti inclite Universitatis Parisiensis, heredibus de Szurkovo, multum pauperis, etc., ille legat comissa, deosque precetur et oret, ut redeat miseris, abeat fortuna superbis). It subsequently came into the possession of the Master Iohannes de Iuniwladislavia (f. 1r: Questiones cum lectura De anima, De ente et essentia et alia magistri Iohannis de Iuniwladislavia).

summary.ab: The different scribes who copied the text do not attribute it to any master. However, after the colophon of the last book, a different hand added: Lectura magistri Roberti Cybolli, in theologia excellentissimi professoris, f. 242r.

Compared to the printed editions and to the manuscript Praha, Knihovna Národního Muzea, XII C 10—the other copy of the QsPol that I have collated—this manuscript contains two additional questions: I.7, f. 20ra: queritur que et quales sunt bona que homines persequuntur ex constitutione politice seu communitatis que 'civitas' nuncupatur; and II.9: Nona questio est que sunt que debet narrare verus rex et que solent tyranni narrare, aut possumus dicere que debet operari verus rex et que facere se tyrannus soleat. The structure of these questions differs from that of the others: they lack contrary arguments, notabilia, conclusions and dubitationes. Instead, they begin simply with unde notandum and are significantly shorter (around four columns and seven lines, i.e. less than half the length of the shortest standard questions). If we accept the note added after the final colophon, according to which this commentary resulted from a lecture by Robert Ciboule, these additions could plausibly originate from him.

msItem.title: QsPol
msItem.colophon: Et hec de presenti questione et de isto octavo libro Politicorum Aristotelis. Explicit octavus liber Politicorum Aristotelis

origin.origDate: The death of Robert Ciboule in 1458 provides a possible terminus ante quem for the manuscript, if this copy indeed derives from a lecture delivered by him.
origin.p: The colophons do not provide any explicit information about the origin or date of the manuscript. However, the reference to the theology master Robert Ciboule in the final colophon suggests a Parisian provenance. This hypothesis is supported by the two-column layout with large squared incipits, a feature found in several Parisian copies of Versoris's texts.

Two parchment guard leaves have been added at the beginning and end of the volume, taken from Book III of Gregory the Great's Decretales. The incipit of the fragment used in the first guard leaf reads: formam visibilem, veritatem corporis, et virtutem spiritualem; the incipit of the fragment used in the second guard leaf reads: Neque per hoc possit augmentatio [sic] probari quod in sacramento eucharistie.

provenance.p: The manuscript belonged to the library of Notre Dame Cathedral in Laon.

listBibl.bibl: Catalogue général des manuscrits des bibliothèques publiques de France. Départements. Tome I: Laon, Paris 1849, p. 231-232.

summary.ab: A parchment title label of the fifteenth century on the front cover reads: Questiones Versoris super Phisicam. Item questiones Metaphysice et super De anima. The ascription to Versoris is also found in the table of contents written in 1466 – the date of the binding’s fabrication – on the inside front cover: In isto libro continentur subscripta: Versoris super Phisicorum, De generatione, Parva naturalia, De celo, Metheororum. De anima, Metaphysica coloniensia. As in the spine label, the layout makes clear that QdA and QsMeta should not be ascribed to Versoris, but are rather colonienses. However, the collation shows that QdC and QdG also do not correspond to the commentaries ascribed to Versoris.

msItem.author: Johannes Versoris
msItem.title: QsP
msItem.colophon: in die conversionis sancti Pauli, anno LXII°
Expliciunt libri Physicorum, anno M° etc. LXII°, octava sancti Laurentii in studio Lipczensi per M. de B

msItem.author: Not specified
msItem.title: QdA
msItem.colophon: in vigilia sancti Martini anno LXII°, libri De anima explicunt in studio Lipczensi per M. de B

origin.p: The manuscript was copied at Leipzig University, as stated in the following colophons: QsP, f. 47rb, f. 108vb; QdA, f. 168va. On an almost unreadable paper spine label from the sixteenth or seventeenth century, the following words can be distinguished: Questiones Versoris librorum Phisicorum, De generatione et corruptione †…† Meteororum. De anima, Metaphysicorum Coloniensium. The layout makes clear that the qualification “Coloniensium” applies only to the last two texts.

provenance.p: The codex was transferred to the holdings of the Leipzig University Library (Bibliotheca Paulina) in 1683, along with the library of the Faculty of Philosophy.

listBibl.bibl: C. Mackert, Die Bibliothek des Augustiner Chorherrenstifts St. Thomas, in: Thomas Fuchs (Hrsg.), 3 x Thomas. Die Bibliotheken des Thomasklosters, der Thomaskirche und der Thomasschule im Laufe der Jahrhunderte. Katalog zur gleichnamigen Ausstellung in der Bibliotheca Albertina vom 18. Oktober 2012 bis 20. Januar 2013, 9–36, at 13–15.

msItem.title: QsE I-VI
msItem.note: The QsE are interrupted at the beginning of the response to question VI.3 (Utrum ars et prudentia sint duae virtutes ab invicem et ab aliis distinctae), with the words: non considerantur contingere.

msItem.title: Albert the Great’s commentary
msItem.note: The QsE are followed by four blank folios, after which we find another question commentary on Aristotle’s Ethics (books I–II), copied by a different hand (ff. 137ra–160vb). This text directly refers to Albert the Great’s commentary.
msItem.incipit: Omnis ars et omnis doctrina. Ad habendam aliqualem notitiam illorum quae doctor noster Albertus Magnus in commento suo circa prooemium moralis philosophiae proponit, dubitatur utrum de virtutibus moralibus sit scientia sicut de intellectualibus
msItem.explicit: et sic concluditur quod malum in ratione mali est infinitum

msItem.title: QsMeta I-VI
msItem.note: The QsMeta break off at question VI.2 (Utrum de ente per accidens sit scientia), with the words: et non semper sequitur quod deficit ut in paucioribus et.
The manuscript also contains texts stemming from the Albertist tradition, namely Heimericus de Campo’s Compendium divinorum and Albert the Great’s De intellectu et intelligibili.

origin.p: The manuscript was copied in Paris, as attested by the colophon to Aristotle’s Nicomachean Ethics, transcribed by Johannes Duss: Explicit liber Ethicorum Aristotelis, finitus Parisius per manus Iohannis Duss, Leodicensis dyocesis, anno Domini M° CCCC° XLVIII° in profesto purificationis Marie, infra nonam et decimam horas, cena peracta (f. 82ra). The watermark found throughout the texts attributed to Versoris is a crossbow, corresponding to Briquet 726 (Troyes, 1447).

summary.ab: Several notes and colophons by the hand of Iohannes Juff ascribe the texts to Versoris: Note before the beginning of CsPH, f. 29r, marg. sup.: Scriptum Versoris, magistri Parisiensis famosissimi, super tractatus Petri Hispani; Note at the beginning of CsPH, f. 30r: Dicta Versoris, famosissimi Parisiensis; Note at the beginning of CsPH, Book VII, f. 215r: Dicta Versoris, famosissimi Parisiensis; QsPri, f. 283v: finis questionum librorum Priorum compilatarum per magistrum Iohannem Versorem; Note at the beginning of QsPos 299r, marg. sup.: Rationes Versoris; QsPos, f. 329v: finis questionum librorum Priorum et Posteriorum Versoris, tunc temporis magistri parisiensis famosissimi. A question commentary ascribed to the Heidelberg master Iohannes Petri is transmitted at ff. 283v–298r. Its colophon reads: finis questionum libri Posteriorum per magistrum Iohannem Petri compilatarum in universitate Heidelbergensi.

msItem.title: CsPH
msItem.colophon: Per me, Iohannem Juff de Butzpach [ alia manu: scripta], in studio Heidelbergensi [alia manu: sed Parisius compilata]
Per me, Iohannem Juff de Butzbach [alia manu: collecta], in studio Heidelbergensi, anno Domini LXII° [alia manu: sed Parisius compilata]

origin.p: The manuscript was copied by Iohannes Juff de Butzbach, a student at Heildeberg University, as stated in the colophons: CsPH, f. 137v; CsPH, f. 251v.

provenance.p: Following its owner, who entered the Carthusian order, the manuscript came into the possession of the convent library.

msItem.title: QdA
msItem.note: It seems not to have been copied by Marcellus Geist.

msItem.title: QdC
msItem.note: It seems not to have been copied by Marcellus Geist.

origin.p: Date as evident from the colophons: Hanc determinavi questionem feria quarta, post decollationem Iohannis Baptiste, anno domini 1449, de mane, presidente magistro Iohanni Buschbach, sui magister unius anni cum dimidio, f. 240ra; Hec questio determinata est per magistrum Marcellum Geyst, anno millesimo quadrigentesimo quinquagesimo, sexta feria ante nativitatis Marie, in tertio anno magistratus ipsius, f. 258va; edita a magistro Marcello, anno LII
This codex contains texts copied by Marcellus Geist, who studied and taught at Heidelberg University until 1452. In 1454, he entered the Carthusian Order and was appointed prior in Mainz in 1470.

provenance.p: After Marcellus death, the codex passed into the possession of the library of the Mainz Carthusians.

listBibl.bibl: Krämer, Handschriftenerbe der Kartäuser im Spiegel ausgewählter Handschriften der Wissenschaftlichen Stadtbibliothek Mainz, 2021, 3, n. 181. https://resolver.staatsbibliothek-berlin.de/HSP0006532A00000000

C. Winterer, Fortsetzung der Katalogisierung der Handschriften der wissenschaftlichen Stadtbibliothek Mainz. Vorläufige Beschreibungen Hs I 491 - Hs I 512 und ab Hs I 530. https://resolver.staatsbibliothek-berlin.de/HSP0006532A00000000

summary.ab: The texts are ascribed to Versoris in the colophons quoted above.

msItem.title: QsSex

origin.p: The texts were copied at the University of Leipzig by Laurentius Sartoris de Sweydnitcz, as stated in the following colophons: QsSex, f. 61rb; QdInt, f. 83rb; Final colophon, f. 209rb.

msItem.title: CsPH
msItem.note: Book I ends unfinished on f. 62v with the treatment of modal propositions bene variantur specie accidentali et ex parte affirmationis et negationis. The scribe wrote afterwards: require in fine libri †. Books V and VI are missing.
Versoris’s commentary alternates with the text of Peter of Spain’s Summulae, which is copied in larger characters with increased interlinear spacing.

origin.p: Date as read at the end of book V of Peter of Spain’s Summulae, f. 193r.

The manuscript was copied and owned by the monk Heinrich Erlbach, from the benedictine monastery of Saint Emmeram in Regensburg. His name appears in the colophons: frater Henricus ordinis divi Benedicti, f. 181v; Heinricus Erlbach, tunc temporis scolaris veteris cappelle Ratisbonensis, f. 274v.

msItem.title: QsP
msItem.note: Ending interrupted at the question Utrum omne quod reflectitur in puncto necesse sit quiescere

origin.origDate: ca. 1461, a dating found in ms. München, BSB, Clm 6694, which presents a watermark identical to this codex. According to the modern description, some of the watermarks are attested between 1453 and 1459.
origin.p: The manuscript was largely copied by a single scribe named Paulus, who also copied MS 557 in the same collection. It contains a watermark with a double key, also found in München, Bayerische Staatsbibliothek, Clm 6694 (WZIS DE5580-Clm6694_72a). Since the latter manuscript probably originated in Heidelberg and is linked by its watermarks to two other manuscripts copied in Heidelberg (Innsbruck, Universitäts- und Landesbibliothek Tirol, Cod. 220, and München, Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München, Universitätsbibliothek, 2 Cod. ms. 557), a Heidelberg origin can probably be extended to the present codex as well.

note.p: München, Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität, Cod. ms. 557, copied in the same period by the same scribe, Paulus, and containing the same watermark with a double key (WZIS DE5580-Clm6694_72a).
Innsbruck, Universitäts- und Landesbibliothek Tirol, Cod. 220 and München, BSB, Clm 6694 which present similar watermarks, possibly indicating a common origin.

provenance.p: The manuscript belonged to the library of the Ingolstadt Arts Faculty, as attested by the ex libris on ff. 1r, 10r, 186v, and on the back cover.

listBibl.bibl: Daniel, Schott, Zahn, Die lateinischen mittelalterlichen Handschriften, 90–91.

msItem.title: QsSE
msItem.colophon: Expliciunt questiones Elencorum Aristotelis. Nutrix Verbigene te laudo agili corde. De libri termino Paulusque pura mente

origin.origDate: ca. 1461, a dating found in ms. München, BSB, Clm 6694, which presents a watermark identical to this codex.
origin.p: No explicit information about the date and origin of this manuscript can be directly inferred from it. The main scribe, named Paulus (as indicated in the colophon, f. 190rb), also copied ms. 568 of this collection. The catalogue’s editors hypothesize that this manuscript and MS 568 originated in Prague, based on the correspondence between the date and the order in which the texts are transmitted and those of the Prague copies, most of which were produced between 1453 and 1459. However, Versoris’s commentaries were copied in many universities during these years. As for the order of the texts, the same sequence characterizes all other copies of the corpus attributed to Versoris. A different origin therefore seems more plausible, namely Heidelberg. In fact, this codex presents the same double key watermark found in ms. Innsbruck, Universitäts- und Landesbibliothek Tirol, Cod. 220 and München, BSB, Clm 6694 (WZIS DE5580-Clm6694_72a), which originated in Heidelberg.

note.p: Innsbruck, Universitäts- und Landesbibliothek Tirol, Cod. 220 and München, BSB, Clm 6694, which present similar watermarks, possibly indicating a common origin. Ms. Clm 6694, f. 188rb also presents the same colophon found at f. 190rb: Nutrix verbigene te laudo agili corde. De libri termino pallasque sereno mente.
München, Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität, Cod. ms. 568, which was copied in the same period by the same scribe, Paulus, and contains the same watermark with a double key (WZIS DE5580-Clm6694_72a).

provenance.p: The manuscript belonged to the library of the Arts Faculty of Ingolstadt University, as attested by the ex libris on ff. 1r, 10r, and on the back cover.

listBibl.bibl: N. Daniel, G. Schott, P. Zahn, Die lateinischen mittelalterlichen Handschriften der Universitätsbibliothek München. Die Handschriften aus der Folioreihe, Zweite Hälfte, Wiesbaden 1979, 79–80.

msItem.title: QsDiv
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis, laudetur Deus in divinis. 1478

origin.origDate: 1478, as stated in the colophon, f. 223ra.
origin.p: The different parts of this composite philosophical manuscript were copied in South Germany between 1472 and 1478.
origin.note: The manuscript also contains texts by Albert the Great and Thomas Aquinas, as well as Albertistic texts, such as Iohannes de Nova Domo’s De universali reali (ff. 130r–131v) and Heymericus de Campo’s Tractatus problematicus (ff. 155v–160v; 126r–130r).

provenance.p: The codex belonged to the library of the Ingolstadt Arts Faculty. It is therefore possible that the manuscript originated at Ingolstadt University.

listBibl.bibl: G. Schott, Die Handschriften der Universitätsbibliothek München. Erster Band: Die deutschen mittelalterlichen Handschriften der Universitätsbibliothek München, Wiesbaden 1968, 169–170.
M. Reuter, Die lateinischen mittelalterlichen Handschriften der Universitätsbibliothek München: die Handschriften aus der Quartreihe, Wiesbaden 2000, 175–180.

on f. 93rb, marg. inf., we find an indication on the teaching practice: Non legitur primus tractatus Metaphysice, quia in ipso solum tractantur et reprobantur opiniones antiquorum de primis principiis et causis entium.

origin.p: The texts were copied by Petrus Lutcz de Schweygern and Johannes Ceyll.
The colophons inform us that the text were copied in 1468 (QsP and QdA), 1471 (QsMeta) and 1477 (QdLong).

Heidelberg, as stated in the colophon on f. 72va: Aratus autem hic est libellus per maystrum Petrum Lutcz de Schweyern, anno millesimo quadrigentesimo septuagesimo sexto, finitusque eodem in die Thome Cantuariensis martii altera, quod est in die Innocentium, in alma universitate Heidelbergensi.

provenance.p: The codex belonged to the Augustinian convent.

msItem.note: The text is acephalus and begins with the words: eius, sicut proprium consequitur speciem (book I). It ends unfinished at book V with the words: ergo quoddam corpus est ubique, quia.
msItem.colophon: Explicit quartus tractatus Petri Hispani de sillogismis, per manum fratris Leonhardi Schroter conventus Ratisponensis

origin.p: The watermark (a unicorn particularly visible in the last quire) is very similar to Piccard X, III, 2136 (Wesel, 1478) and 2137 (Wesel 1475).
Regensburg, as stated in the colophon on f. 93ra.

summary.ab: No. The QsP are presented as having been written under the direction of Master Clémens Parmentier.

msItem.title: QsMeta
msItem.note: only books I–II, unfinished

physDesc.additions: The manuscript contains several long marginal notes in which Averroes, Albert, and Thomas are quoted.

origin.p: Folios 1–163 were written mostly by Galcerandus Benedicti in 1446–1447; folios 164–193, written by Gilbertus Amignacensis, are undated; folios 194–266 were written mostly by Johannes Run in 1443. The extensive colophons provide information about the date and place of the copy, as well as the identity of the scribes and the master supervising them: QsP, book I, f. 14ra; QsP, book III, f. 36ra; QsP, book IV, f. 51rb; QsP, book V, f. 59rb; QdA, book III, f. 232rb; Final colophon, f. 255rb

The texts contained in this codex were copied by several students at the Paris Arts Faculty: Galcerandus Benedicti (see below); Gilbertus Armignac (f. 184rb: sequitur figura ventorum in sequenti folio, Deo Gilbertus Armignac); Reginaldus Eude (f. 196ra: Et hoc de questione et per consequens de toto primo libro De anima, finito in vico Straminis. Reginaldus Eude); and Johannes Run, writing for Iohannes Colomines (see below).
origin.note: Oxford, Bodleian Library, Canon. Misc. 486, also copied by Iohannes Run, and Saint Omer, Bibliothèque d’Agglomération du Pays de Saint-Omer, 586, also written under the supervision of the master Clémens Parmentier.

history.acquisition: The codex was purchased by the Bodleian in 1817.

listBibl.bibl: Coxe, Catalogi, Quarto Catalogues III, col. 581–582.

Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto tertio libro Methaphisice Aristotelis, completo Parisius, secunda die mensis iunii, I. Q.

Et hec de questione presenti, et per consequens de toto quarto libro Methaphisice Aristotilis, completo Parisius, per me, Iohannem Quarreti, scolarem Parisius, studentem in artium facultati, anno Domini millesimo quadrigentesimo quadragesimo sexto

msItem.title: QsE I–VI
msItem.note: The QsE stops unfinished at f. 169v, question VI.7, Utrum synesis et gnomin sunt virtutes distincte adiuncte prudentie, with the words: sciendum est tertio, pro ampliori.

origin.p: 1446, as attested in the colophon of Book IV of the QsMeta: Et hec de questione presenti, et per consequens de toto quarto libro Methaphisice Aristotilis, completo Parisius, per me, Iohannem Quarreti, scolarem Parisius, studentem in artium facultati, anno Domini millesimo quadrigentesimo quadragesimo sexto, f. 54v.

The manuscript was copied in Paris by the student Iohannes Quarreti, as attested in the colophons of books I and III of the QsMeta: Et hec de questione presenti, et per consequens de toto primo libro Methaphisice Aristotelis, scripto et completo per me, Iohannem Quarreti, venerabilis nationis Francie, vicesima die mai, f. 12ra; Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto tertio libro Methaphisice Aristotelis, completo Parisius, secunda die mensis iunii, I. Q., f. 34va.

listBibl.bibl: Rouse, Rouse, Illiterati et uxorati II,84

summary.ab: The text is ascribed to Versoris in a note on the upper margin of f. 152r, written by the copyist: Questiones Versoris super sex libros Ethicorum. Bénédicte Sère tentatively ascribes the Questions on the Ethics transmitted at ff. 152r–215v to the master Wilhelm of Walma. However, Gauthier attributes these Questions to Versoris, suggesting that Wilhelm of Walma merely used Versoris’s Questions for his lectures.

msItem.title: QsE I–VI
msItem.note: Anonymous set of questions on the Ethics on books I–VI (ff. 2ra–151vb).

msItem.title: QsE I–VI
msItem.note: The text is ascribed to Versoris in a note on the upper margin of f. 152r, written by the copyist: Questiones Versoris super sex libros Ethicorum.

This commentary is preceded by an anonymous set of questions on the Ethics, again only on books I–VI (ff. 2ra–151vb). Interestingly, the last leaf of the commentary contains some notes on the subject matter of the seventh and the eighth book of Aristotle’s Ethics: De materia septimi libri, qui est de continentia et incontinentia, et de delectatione et tristitia, ut sunt earum obiecta [… ] Hoc Sanctus Thomas, prima secunde, questio 26, articulo tertio. […], f. 215va; In octavo huius. Amicitia est benivolentia qua bonum alteri voluimus […] Amicitia propter honestum est perfecta, f. 215vb. See Seńko, Repertorium, vol. 1, p. 108–111.
msItem.incipit: Circa primum librum Ethicorum Aristotelis movetur talis questio primo, utrum de virtutibus moralibus sit scientia. Et arguitur primo quod non: notitia que habetur de virtutibus moralibus est prudentia; ergo non est scientia. Consequentia tenet, quia scientia et prudentia sunt habitus distincti specie, ex sexto Ethicorum
msItem.explicit: quia prius oportet ostendere quod istud possit generare hominem
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis sexti Ethicorum, finiti per me, Claudium Verneti, dyocesis Bisuntiensis, studentem sub venerabili viro, magistro Galthero de Walma, actu Parisius regente in collegio Burgundie, in die Iovis penitentiali, hora quasi decima post prandium, anno Domini millesimo quadragentesimo sexagesimo tertio

origin.p: The colophon of the second set of questions on the Ethics informs us that it was copied by Claudius Vernet under the supervision of Galtherus de Walma, regent master at the Collège de Bourgogne: Et sic est finis sexti Ethicorum, finiti per me, Claudium Verneti, dyocesis Bisuntiensis, studentem sub venerabili viro, magistro Galthero de Walma, actu Parisius regente in collegio Burgundie, in die Iovis penitentiali, hora quasi decima post prandium, anno Domini millesimo quadragentesimo sexagesimo tertio, f. 215v.
origin.note: Berlin, SB – PK, lat. qu. 435; Wrocław, BU, Ms. IV F 1, also copied in the Collège de Bourgogne.

Gauthier, L'Éthique à Nicomaque, p. 141, fn 166, p. 142, fn. 170

P.J.J.M. Bakker, F.J. Kok, Secundum intentionem Doctoris Subtilis: The Commentaries on Porphyry's Isagoge and Aristotle‘s De anima by Walter of Wervia (ca. 1425–1497), Bulletin de Philosophie Médiévale 56 (2014), 263-279, at 264, n. 3.

msItem.title: QsP
msItem.note: Beginning at q.II.8 with the words vel motus ad formam, sed est causa per accidens
The beginning of the QsP is missing

msItem.title: QdG
msItem.note: Some folios have been ripped

msItem.title: QsSom
msItem.note: It ends interrupted with the words apparet nobis sit vere

msItem.title: QdLon
msItem.note: The QdLon are missing

layout.p: The manuscript presents the typical layout of Parisian volumes of the mid-fifteenth century: it is written in two columns; at the beginning of each commentary, the scribe left a quadrangular space intended to be filled with a squared initial, which, however, was never executed and remains blank.

origin.origDate: Undated
origin.p: The manuscript is undated. A watermark appears throughout the volume—a fleur-de-lis (particularly visible on ff. 39, 59, 65, 66, 120–121, 137, 149, 207)—similar to Briquet 6911, attested in Paris in 1457, with a similar variant recorded in 1460. Another watermark, an anchor (visible on f. 101), is very similar to Briquet 391, attested in Bagé in 1452, as well as to Piccard II 516, attested in Brugges in 1452, and 517, attested in Mehelen in 1456.

The manuscript was copied by a single student, Simon de Horreo, in Paris, at the school of the master Iohannes Hameron, as attested by the colophons: QsP, final colophon, f. 82v; QdC, book I, 101rb; QdC, final colophon, 120vb; QsMete, final colophon, 223vb.
origin.note: Simon de Horreo highlights that the order in which the commentaries are copied does not correspond to the one in which they were taught, and reestablishes the correct order in two notes: Item secundum dictum ordinem librorum tres libri Metheororum qui iam hic sequuntur, immediate deberent precedere tres libros De anima et immediate sequi duos libros De generatione et corruptione, f. 193rb; and again, at the end of the QsM: Item notatur quod post istos tres libros sic completos bono ordine et congruo debent sequi tres libri De anima, et isti immediate sequuntur duos libros De generatione et corruptione, licet in compositione libri male observatur, f. 223v.

listBibl.bibl: R. Nir, “Rękopisy filozoficzne Biblioteki Seminarium Duchownego w Pelplinie”, Studia Gdańskie 3 (1978), 185–213, at 197, n. 44.
W. Seńko,Rękopisy Biblioteki Seminarium Duchownego w Pelplinie, Warszawa 1969, 76.

msItem.title: QsInt
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto libro, in quo libro sunt septem lectiones […], sub venerabili magistro Girardo de Watrellet, actu regenti Parisius, in domo magistri Gaufridi Calvi

msItem.title: QsPri
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto primo libro Priorum, finito per manum Yvonis de Beisit, sub venerabili ac discreto viro, magistro Girardo de Watrellet, in vico straminis, commoranti in domo magistri Gaufridi Calvi

Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto primo [sic] libro Priorum, finito per manum Yvonis de Beisit, sub venerabili ac discreto viro, magistro Girardo de Watrellet, in vico straminis, commoranti in domo magistri Gaufridi Calvi. Et fuit finitus iste liber scribendo octava die mensis aprilis, anno Domini Mmo CCCCmo LImo, Yvo de Beisit fecit hoc, .Scriptor qui scripsit cum Christo vivere possit. Si scriptor voluisset [add. sup. lin.: et potuisset] melius scripsisset, sed quia non potuit scribere non potuit

msItem.title: QsPos
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto primo libro Posteriorum, finito per manum Yvonis de Beisit, in domo magistri Gaufridi Calvi, sub venerabili magistro ac reverendo magistro Girardo Watrellet, in prima die mensis iunii, anno Domini Domini Mmo CCCCmo LImo

Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto secundo libro Posteriorum, scripto per me, Yvonem de Beisit, sub venerabili magistro Girardo de Watrellet, in domo magistri Gaufridy Calvi. Et fuit liber completus in mense iunii, in vigilia sancti Iohannis, anno Domini Mmo CCCCmo LImo. Yvo de Beisit. Scriptor qui scripsit cum Christo vivere possit, detur pro pena scriptoris pulchra puella. Scriptor scripsisset melius si voluisset, sed quia non potuit, scribere ipse noluit. Nomen scriptoris, Yvo Beisit plenus amoris. [alia manu: reverendissimo meo avunculo, magistro Yvoni Kentel dedi. Scriptor scribebat ex ‘I’ bene ‘o’ faciebat, pennam deposuit, et ‘o’ velud ante fuit, Iudicellus Rouyant] On the formula, “detur pro pena scriptoris ...”, see Reynhout, Formules latines des colophons, I, 116-125; on the formula “nomen scriptoris, X plenus amoris”, 186-194.

msItem.title: QsTop I-IV
msItem.colophon: Et fuit finitus iste liber describendo in octava die mensis aprilis, sub venerabili magistro Girardo de Watrellet, in domo magistri Gaufridi Calvi actu regentis Parisius. Scriptor qui scripsit cum Christo vivere possit

Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto secundo libro Topicorum. Et fuit iste liber completus per me, Yvonem de Beisit, in domo magistri Gaufridi Calvi, sub venerabili magistro Girardo Watrellet, in fine mensis aprilis, anno Domini Mmo CCCCmo LImo”

Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto quarto libro Topicorum, completo per manum Yvonis de Beisit, in domo magistri Gaufridi Calvi, sub venerabili magistro Girardo de Watrellet. Et Deus habeat animam scriptoris et etiam magistri. Et fuit completus liber prima die mensis iuliiThe word iulii appears twice, each time crossed out; iunii was later inserted above the line, yet the date given in the preceding colophon indicates that iulii is the correct reading., anno Domini Mmo CCCCmo LImo. Yvo de Beisit”

msItem.title: QsSE
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto primo Elenchorum, scripto [209vb] sub venerabili magistro Girardo de Watrellet, actu regente Parisius, in domo magistri Gaufridi Calvi, in vicesimam secundam mensis iulii, anno Domini Mmo CCCCmo LImo

origin.p: The manuscript was copied by Yvo de Beisit, a student of Gerardus de Watrellet in the school of Gaufridus Calvi, as attested by the numerous colophons: QsInt, f. 96v; QsPri, book I, f. 116rb; QsPri,final colophon, f. 126va; QsPos, book I, f. 153vb; QsPos, final colophon: f. 164va; QsT, book I, f. 174vb; QsT, book II, f. 181vb; QsT, book IV, f. 185rb; QsSE, book I, f. 209va-b.

provenance.p: The manuscript later belonged to Iudicellus Rouyant, a Breton student at the Collège de Tréguier, who in 1456 transferred it to the master Reginaldus Du Brule as a pledge for tuition owed: Ista logica pertinet Iudicello Rouyant, scolari Britoni, commoranti in collegio de Triguier Parisius, quam tradidit magistro Reginaldo du Brule iuniori in pignus pro somma pecunie debita dicto du Brule per prefatum Iudicellum pro instructione et doctrina sucepta Parisius per prefatum Iudicellum in pedagogio dicti du Brule pro temporibus dimidii anni incipientis in festo nativitatis Iohannis Baptiste, anno Domini millesimo quadragentesimo LVIto, f. 1r. The manuscript was then returned to Iudicellus, who gave him to his uncle, Yvo Kentel, as recorded on f. 164va: reverendissimo meo avunculo, magistro Yvoni Kentel dedi. Scriptor scribebat: ex ‘I’ bene ‘o’ faciebat, pennam deposuit, et ‘o’ velud ante fuit, Iudicellus Rouyant.
Throughout the codex, additional ownership inscriptions are found: Iste liber pertinet Iohannes Geoffroy; Iohannes Ryetis, f. 36r; Ista logica pertinet Iohanni Laussardy, f. 228r.

listBibl.bibl: C. Samaran, R. Marichal, Catalogue des manuscrits en écriture latine portant des indications de date, de lieu ou de copiste, Tome III, Bibliothèque Nationale, fonds latin Nos. 8001 à 18613, Paris, 1974, 507.

origin.p: 1451, as stated at f. 128va (A. Kernynyven, Anno Domini M°CCCC° LI°, ) and at f. 359va (Anno Domini M°CCCC° LI°).

The whole manuscript was copied by Alanus Kernynyven, student at Paris Arts Faculty, who signed at the end of the works (end of QdC, f. 172va; end of QdG, f. 227vb; QsSen, f. 245vb; QdMem, f. 250vb; QdSom, f. 357rb), of the books (book I of QdC, f. 153ra, where appears also another name: Getholohan; book II of QsMete, f. 256va; book I of QdA, f. 273va), or of the questions (QdC, q. II.13, f. 158rb). On the inferior guard leafs other names can be read, probably new owners of the manuscript: De Ruppe, abbas; Jehan Mares.

listBibl.bibl: Catalogue général des manuscrits des bibliothèques publiques de France, Départements: 25. Poitiers – Valenciennes, Paris 1894, 46–47.

msItem.title: QsMeta I–XII
msItem.note: This commentary is followed by several writings related to the Utraquist polemics — Hilarius de Litomericz’s already mentioned Disputatio – Hilarius’s letter against Koranda on the communion (ff. 332r–333v, inc. Argute, augur, quaeris) – and a writing from the opposing faction, namely Jan Rokycana’s De communione sub duplici specie (ff. 349r–368r).

origin.p: Around 1465 as the manuscript contains a disputatio held in that year by Hilarius de Litomericz against the head of the Utraquists, Jan Rokycana (Disputatio capituli Pragensis cum Rokycana habita coram rege anno 1465, f. 335r-348v) Hilarii Litomericensis Sancte Ecclesiae Pragensis Decani Disputatio cum Ioanne Rokyczana coram Georgio rege Bohemiae per quinque dies habita anno MCCCCLXV. Nunc cum manuscripto codice coaevo archivi metropolitani capituli Pragensis diligenter collata et emendata novisque post Henricum Canisium et Iacobum Basnagium observationibus illustrata, Litteris Ioannis Caroli Hraba, Inclyti regni Bohemiae D.D. Statuum Typographi, Pragae, 1775.

summary.ab: The texts are not ascribed to a particular master, but Hilarius emphasizes their Parisian origin and their doctrinal orientation in line with Albert the Great and Thomas Aquinas.

msItem.title: QsP
msItem.colophon: Finis secundi Phisicorum, anno 50. Hoc illuminationis tempore devastata Misna dure et acriter, dominus Georgius cum suis Bohemiam revertitur, sed heu pauperibus panis lacrimarum mixtus sangwine in Misna degentibus arctior gladio efficitur

Explicit materia seriatim collecta secundum intentionem Aristotelis super octo libros ipsius .... terminata per Hilarium de Lithomerzic, baccalarium artium liberalium, in collegio sacratissime virginis Dei et Domini nostri genitricis Marie nationis Bohemorum, in domo Reczek, anno Domini 1450

msItem.title: Qdent
msItem.colophon: finitus est iste libellus in die conversionis sancti Pauli, doctoris gentium eximii, anno Christi millesimo quadrigentesimo quinquagesimo secundo, per magistrum Hilarium de Lithomerzicz, licet non ex toto, ut ipse potes manum dinoscere. In collegio sanctissime marie virginis gloriose nationis Bohemorum in domo Reczek

origin.p: The texts were copied at the College of Reczek at Prague University by the students Hilarius de Litomericz and Wenceslaus de Krzizanow. Hilarius transcribed an exemplar written by Wenceslaus de Urbem – now lost – which was among the earliest manuscripts of Versoris's text and one of the first to arrive in Prague.

The colophon on f. 252r provides detailed information about the circumstances of the copy. Detailed information on the (political) context of the copy can be found also in other colophons: colophon of QsP, book I, f. 38r; final colophon of QsP, f. 111v; colophon of Qdent, f. 262va.

summary.ab: The manuscript also contains an excerpt from Nicolaus Bonetus's Commentary on Metaphysics (ff. 332r–344r), copied in 1454.

msItem.title: QdDiv
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis in die sancti Blasii […] post tactum hore decime, anno Domini M°CCCC°LIII°

origin.origDate: 1453, as stated in the colophon of the QdDiv (f. 123v).

msItem.title: CsPH V–VII

origin.origDate: 1480, as stated in the colophon (f. 102v).
origin.p: The manuscript was copied by Iohannes de Plana in Plzeň.

note.p: Praha, KMK, L 39, which also belonged to Alex de Trzebon.

provenance.p: The codex belonged to Alex de Trzebon, as attested by the possession mark on f. 1r: Alexii Baccalarii.

listBibl.bibl: Meirinhos, Bibliotheca manuscripta Petri Hispani, 355–356 ascribing the manuscript to Versoris with doubts.

msItem.title: QsPor
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis impositus, anno Domini, M° CCCC° L°, feria tertia post Laurenti

msItem.title: QsPre
msItem.colophon: Ineffabilis excellentissime bonitatis divinatisque inestimabilis excollatione ad resumptionis extremum questionum libri presentis pertingentes que peracta est subtiliter et admodum ingeniose per reverendum magistrum Stanislaus de Welwara, qui ob suavissimorum dulcissimorumque fructuum vehementem amorem cum laborum fatigationumque magnis impensis durissimum prolixumque iter arripiens, multis calamitatibus oppressum, ad parisiense studium peragravit, ibidemque citra duos annos mansionem surripiens propter eius diligentem studii insudationem corona magistri coronatus est [98v]. Huius libri extremum resumptionis per suprascriptum magistrum adepti sumus anno M CCCC L II in die Donati. Et eadem die consodalis fraterque dilectissimus meus, F. K., licentiam a consodalibus nationis Bohemorum domus Reczek accipiens, religionem demum ingressus est ordinis Slaworum

msItem.title: QsSex
msItem.colophon: hec enim peracta sunt currente anno Domini M° CCCC° L II°, dieque feria secunda post Gregorii. Tunc temporis etiam finitum est examen baccalarium studii Pragensis

msItem.title: QdDiv
msItem.colophon: anno Domini M CCCC L, in die Apollinaris, hora vigesima

msItem.title: QsPri
msItem.colophon: fertia tertia post assumptionem beatissime Marie virginis, anno Domini M CCCC L, hora vigesima quarta

origin.origDate: 1450–1452, as can be inferred from the colophons: colophon of QsPor, f. 50r; colophon of QsPre, f. 98r; colophon of QsSex, f. 112r; colophon of QdDiv, f. 157r; colophon of QsPri, f. 205v.
origin.p: This manuscript was used for teaching at the College Reczek of the Prague Arts Faculty by Master Stanislaus de Velvary. A colophon mentions his stay in Paris, where he first copied the texts transmitted in this manuscript (f. 98r).

note.p: Praha, KMK, M 81; Praha, NK, X H 21; Wien, Dominikanerkonvent, Cod. 149/119, in which Stanislaus de Velvary is mentioned.

listBibl.bibl: Green-Pedersen, The Tradition of the Topics in the Middle Ages, 400.

summary.ab: The texts are ascribed to Versoris in the final colophon at f. 365r and in the table of contents at f. 370v, both by the hand of Crux de Telcz.

msItem.title: QsLon
msItem.colophon: Et in hoc terminatur expositio librorum Philosophi naturalis philosophie, edita questionatim per magistrum Iohannem dictum Versor in studio alme universitatis Parisiensis, allataque per magistrum Wenceslaum de Wrben, adhuc eo pro tunc baccalario existente, finita per Crucem de Telcz, studentem in collegio regis Wenceslai, feria tertia in vinculis Sancti Petri, anno 1459 [1 VIII 1459]

finis titulorum et questionum Iohannis Versoris super libros Aristotelis naturalis philosophie parvorum naturalium per me, fratrem Crucem de Telcz, in monasterio Trzebonensi sub abbate Marco, priore Iohanne Tissnow, anno Domini 1495 in die Panthaleonis, in vigilia Marthe [28 VII 1495]. [alia manu] De iuventute et senectute, De inspiratione et respiratione, De vita et morte, De motu animalium, istos tractatus quinque Parvorum naturalium non habentur in his questionibus Versoris.

origin.origDate: The texts were copied in 1459. In 1495, in Třeboň, Crux added an index at the end of the volume (f. 370v).
origin.p: The manuscript was entirely copied by Crux de Telcz at King Wenceslas College in Prague. Colophons at: QsP book IV, f. 83r; QsP book V, f. 95r; QsP book VII, f. 119r; QsP final, f. 141v; QdC, f. 194v; QdG, f. 227v; QsMete book I, f. 246v; QsMete book II, f. 256v; QsMete final, f. 263r; QdA book I, f. 269r; QdA book II, f. 313v; QdA final, f. 333r; QsSen, f. 350v; QsMem, f. 355r; final colophon, f. 365r; table of contents, f. 370v.

note.p: Praha, NK, I F 25, also copied by Crux de Telcz at the same place and time.

summary.ab: The ascription to Versoris can be found in the initium of table of contents Incipiunt tituli questionum primi libri Metaphysice Aristotelis Iohannis Versoris and in its colophon (f. 304v).

The manuscript, of a metaphysical content, also transmits Johannes Wenck's Questions on the Liber de causis (ff. 290v–302v); Gilles of Rome's Theoremata de esse et essentia and some excerpts from Johannes de Nova Domo's treatise De universali reali.

msItem.title: QsMeta I–XII

origin.p: The most part of the manuscript was copied by Crux de Telcz at King Wenceslas College in Prague.

note.p: Praha, NK, I E 38, also copied by Crux de Telcz at the same place and time.
