Et in hoc completur materia continens summam trium librorum de meteorologicis impressionibus, anni Domini M CCCC 54

origin.origDate: 1453–1454, as attested by the colophons: QsP book I, f. 21v; book II, f. 37r; book III, f. 47r; book IV, f. 67v; book V, f. 77r; book VI, f. 88r; book VII, f. 94v; QsP final, f. 110v; QdA, f. 156v; QdSen, f. 171r; QdSom, f. 180v; QdLon, f. 182v; QdC book I, f. 203v; book II, f. 222r; QdC final, f. 225v; QdG book I, f. 241v; QdG final, f. 251v; QsMete book I, f. 265v; book II, f. 273r; QsMete final, f. 278v.
origin.p: The manuscript was copied at the College Nazareth of Prague University.

summary.ab: The manuscript also contains some excerpts from Gilles of Rome's Theoremata de esse et essentia (ff. 1r–3r), a fragment from Heymericus de Campo's Tractatus problematicus (ff. 10r–29r), and some (pseudo)-Thomistic texts (ff. 203r–213v).

origin.origDate: 1455, as evident from the following two colophons: Et sic est finis, feria secunda ante Laurentii, quo die fuit coniunctio Lune cum Scorpione, 1455, f. 9v; A quodam bone memorie Martino Polono in scola sancti Egidii me presente ibi anno etc. 1455, f. 134r.
origin.p: The manuscript was copied in Prague, mostly by the master Stanislaus de Gnezna (ff. 30–213), who signed at f. 132v: Et tantum de questione et per consequens de toto libro et ipsius resumpcione per me, reverendum magistrum Stanislaum (...) post sigillationem sancti Wenceslai.

listBibl.bibl: P. Rutten, Introduction to the edition of Heymericus de Campo's Tractatus problematicus (unpublished), chapter 2.6, Manuscript P2, 24–29. https://drive.google.com/file/d/13EgNUnMYVOTBxCR-4qGuiPP1lFzbDdG8/view

msItem.title: QsTop I-IV
msItem.colophon: finitus in domo Reczkonis, in vigilia beate Marie, sexta feria anno 1456

Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto quarto libro Topicorum Aristotelis. Finitus est feria quarta post resurrectionem dominicam in collegio sanctissime virginis marie Nationis Bohemorum, in domo felicis memorie Reczek, et est finitus ante prandium, anno Domini M CCC L VI

msItem.title: QsSE
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto libro Elenchorum. Finito libro sit laus et gloria Christo, amen. Et est finitus in domo Reczkonis in capite ieiunii, hora quartadecima, anno Domini 1456

origin.p: The texts were copied in the College Reczek in Prague, as evident from the colophons: QsTop, book II, f. 210r; QsTop, final colophon, f. 221r; QsSE, final colophon, f. 267r. The colophon of the Tractatus de intentionibus praedicabilium (f. 221v–233v) specifies that these texts were copied in 1450 by the master Stanislaus in Paris, in the school of master Albertus Scriptoris, and from there carried to Prague: iste est ergo ortulus, qui Zabulum Porphyrii irrigare videtur, qui portatus est per reverendum magistrum Stanislaum de Parisius, quem finivit ibidem in pedagogio magistri Alberti Scriptoris, et hoc anno Domini 1450. Et est finitus in collegio Reczek ante dominicam Iudica anno 1457.

Some notes throughout the codex prove that these commentaries were adopted by Prague masters for their teaching. See for instance f. 3r: Magister reverendus Wenczeslaus incepit legere vetere artem feria quarta ante festum Galli”; f. 238r: Incepit librum Elenchorum legere fertia sexta proxima post Margarethe, in lectorio Platonis, hora undecima. The master could be Wenceslaus de Paczow, who taught Versoris’s commentaries transmitted in the Prague manuscripts NKČR, IV F 8 and X G 1.

note.p: Stuttgart, Württembergische Landesbibliothek, HB X 2 copied in the school of Albertus Scriptoris.
Praha, KMK, M 40/2; Praha, KMK, M 81; Wien, Dominikanerkonvent, Cod. 149/119 in which Stanislaus de Velvary is mentioned.

summary.ab: From 1459 to 1496, these commentaries were used for teaching by several Prague masters, whose names and lecture dates are recorded in the marginalia throughout the codex, particularly at the beginning of the commentaries: f. 1r, marg. sup.: Magister Paulus orsus est librum veteris artis feria secunda ante Elisabeth, anno 1477 [17 XI 1477] et finivit 1478, feria tertia post Francisci, hora quartadecima, in magno lectorio, hora quindecima [6 X 1478]; [alia manu, rubeo]: Magister Martinus, artium facultatis decanus, orsus est librum veteris artis feria secunda post Dorothee, hora quartadecima, anno 1496 [8 II 1496]; f. 143(bis)v, marg. sup.: anno Domini millesimo 492 reverendus magister Mathias de Pielgrzymow incepit legere librum Posteriorum; f. 144r, marg. sup. (difficult to read): Magister de Vi(?) incepit in lectorio, feria ?, hora 13, 1479; f. 245v, marg. inf.: anno etc. LVIII magister Iohannes de Praga incepit legere puncta cum textu Priorum, feria secunda post Matheus apostoli [24 IX 1459]; back guard leaf: anno 1466 magister Wenczeslaus de Paczow incepit primum librum Posteriorum, feria quarta ante Stanislai, hora 11 [7 V 1466], quem terminavit feria secunda post Francisci, hora quartadecima, in lectorio theologorum [6 X 1466].

msItem.title: QsMeta I–VI
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto primo libro, scripto per Iohannem Abbatis, tunc temporis in collegio Marchie existentem, in festo sancti Dyonisiis, hora vespertina, Abbatis
Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto secundo, scripto per me, Iohannem Abbatis, venerande [22ra] nationis Francie, dyocesis Remensis, anno Domini 1452, Abbatis
Et hec de questione, et per consequens de libro tertio, scripto per Iohannem Abbatis, anno Domini millesimo CCCC° quinquagesimo secundo, Abbatis

Et hec de questione, [65rb] et per consequens de sexto libro Metaphysice Aristotelis, scripto per me, Iohannem de Remis, venerande nationis Francie, provincieque dyocesis ville Remensis, anno Domini 1452 in die sancti Stephani prothomartiris. Anno Domini 1453 [sic]. Scriptor qui scripsit cum Christo vivere possit. Da, pater egregie, michi carpere dogmata fronte, servulus indignus si poscit hoc, esto benignus. Iohannes Abbatis

msItem.title: QsE I–VI
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de secundo libro Ethicorum, scripto per me, Iohannem Abbatis, Parisius, in festo sancte Crucis
Et hec de questione, et per consequens de tertio libro Ethicorum, scripto per me, Iohannem Abbatis, Parisius, anno Domini millesimo CCCC° 5°, die feriali?, decimanona mensis septembris, Abbatis
Et hec de questione, et per consequens [de] toto quinto libro, scripto in vigilia sancti Michaelis, per Iohannem Abbatis, anno Domini millesimo CCCC° 5°. Abbatis

Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto libro sexto Ethicorum, scripto per me, Iohannem Abbatis, tunc temporis commorantem in venerabili collegio Marchie, completoque quinta die mensis Octobris, in crastino sancti Francisci, anno Domini millesimo CCCC° primo. Explicit hoc totum, pro pena da mihi potum. Qui scribere nescit nullum putat esse laborem. Tres digiti scribunt, cetera membra dolent.

msItem.title: Sincathegereumata
msItem.note: To these colophons of the texts ascribed to Versoris, we can add the other one by Johannem Abbatis.
msItem.colophon: Expliciunt Sincathegereumata magistri Petri Hyspani, Parisius scripta per me Johannem Abbatis, in collegio Marchie, anno Domini Mo CCCCo quinquagesimo, ultima die augusti. Heu male finivi, quia non bene scribere scivi.

origin.p: The manuscript was copied in Paris at the Collège de la Marche by Iohannes Abbatis, as attested by the numerous colophons. Dating attested by the numerous colophons providing rich information on the place, date and identity of the copyist.

provenance.p: On a guard-leaf pasted to the inner front cover we read: "Abbatis", and we find his signum. At f. 168v, we read: "Maistre Jehan Labbé". In the ornamented initial letter at f. 74r, we read: "I. Labbé". The manuscript was later acquired by a certain Iohannes de Bouberto, as attested in a possession mark at f. 168v: "Pertinet Johanni de Bouberto, testantibus Jo[hanne] de Piloriis, Petro de Suessione, J. de Montibus".

listBibl.bibl: Catalogue général des manuscrits des bibliothèques publiques de France. Départements. Tome XXXIX. Reims, vol. II.1, Paris 1904, 210–211
Y.-F. Riou, Alfarabi, Aristote, Platon, Plutarque et Ptolémée à Reims vers 1479, Scriptorium, 49/1 (1995), 106–133, 116–118 who considers the scribe as the author of the commentaries

msItem.title: CsPH, II-III
msItem.note: On f. 1v, we find a list of Italian mottos, beginning with this one: Chi vuole de ogni cossa essere vincente / cum humilta se trouve patiente / chi spende piu che soa borsa non rende / non se l’abia amare se poverta lo prende. After this list, we find a note on Albert the Great’s conception of universals: nota, secundum Albertum, quod universale est triplex, scilicet ante rem, post re et in re. Universale ante rem est ipsa prima causa, comprehendens omnia tanta potentialiter in se ipsa; universale in re est ipsa natura communis induta aliqua materia et quantitate, particularizata in hoc et in hoc subiecto; universale post rem est ipsa natura terminis denudata a tali materia et quantitate subiecti que, postea apprehenditur ab intellectu per conceptum generis et speciei, habet formare de ipso qui postea conceptus, iunctis primis vocatur ‘secunda intentio’. Nota etiam: universale habet duplex esse, scilicet formale et materiale. Esse formale et obiective est quando habet esse in intellectu tantum; esse materiale et fundamentale est esse in suius singularibus.
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis Antepredicamentorum Aristotelis secundum lecturam parisiensem scriptam per me, fratrem Petrum Iohannem Balbum Ianuensem [scripsit: Ianuenses, sed correx.], ordinis predicatorum, in die Epiphanie, M°CCCC°LVIII°, hora XVIII
msItem.note: On f. 56rb, a short sentence schematically represents the Aristotelian categories: Aristoteles (sup. lin.: id est, substantia), magnus (quantitas), Philosophus (qualitas), Sifronici filius (relatio), disputando (actio), fatigatus (passio), in scolis (ubi), hodie (quando), sedet (situatus) coronatus (habitus).
Transmitted anonymously, but the colophon at f. 25ra specifies its Parisian origin. Versoris’s QsPH are not identified in Meirinhos, Bibliotheca, 399-400.

msItem.author: Franciscus de Prato
msItem.title: Incipit liber de intentionibus, fratris Francisci de Pratris, ordinis predicatorum
msItem.incipit: Que est necessitas ponenda speciem, secundum Philosophum in primo celi et mundi

msItem.title: QsApos
msItem.incipit: Circa primum librum Posteriorum Aristotilis movetur question talis, que est in forma talis, videlicet utrum de demonstration sit scientia tamquam de subiecto huius libri Posteriorum. Et arguitur primo quod non contra primum punctum tituli questionis, quia scientia est ingenerabilium et incorruptibilium, sed demonstrations sunt generabils est corruptibiles
msItem.colophon: Questiones sequentes super libro Posteriorum fuerunt composite per reverendum magistrum Richaldum de Bovillia ordinis predicatorum, artium et theologie doctorem

Et hec sufficiant de isto primo libro Posteriorum, quem composuit magister Richaldus de Bovilla, artium doctor eggregius [sic] et scripsit eum fratrer Petrus Iohannes Balbus ordinis predicatorum cum magna festinatione, M°CCCC°LVIII°, die XXI° Iunii, hora decima, cui domino sit merces hic per gratiam et in futuro per gloriam. Amen, in conventu nostro Papiensi
Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto libro primo Posteriorum et secundo, lectis a magistro Richaldo de Bovilla, regente in facultate artium studii Dolanensis procuratorisque eiusdem
msItem.note: The introduction and the colophon of book I qualifie them as “composed” (composite) by the master Richaldus de Bovilla. They are presented as “thaught” (lecte) by this master in the colophon of book II. This information is confirmed by the collation. In fact, neither the incipit nor the list of question corresponds to Versoris’s text.

msItem.title: de demonstratione secundum sanctum Thomam
msItem.note: Treatise on demonstration
msItem.incipit: restat nunc dicere de demonstratione, cum enim scientia sit habitus conclusionis demonstate acquisitus ex ipsius speculatione

msItem.title: Incipit tractatus de reali et ente rationis secundum sacntum Thomam de Aquino ordinis predicatorum
msItem.incipit: Ut scias quid sit ens rationis et quid ens reale

msItem.title: Qdente
msItem.note: The Qdente are presented as “composed” (composite) by Versoris: Explicit liber utilis de ente et essentia compositus a magistro Iohanne Versoris supra textum sancti Thome de Aquino ordinis predicatorum, f. 115v.

msItem.title: QsP I-VI
msItem.note: At the end of book VII of the QsP, the scribe wrote that no copy of books VII-VIII was available to him: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de libro sexto Phisicorum finis omnibus. Et notetis quod non scribo alios duos libros, scilicet septimum et octavum, quia illorum copiam deficit, f. 193ra. The text is followed by a list of the most important propositions of the commentary (f. 193va: Iste sunt propositiones primi libri Phisicorum).

msItem.title: QdA
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis secundi libri De anima [erasit: per me fratrem Francischum de Rippa] ordinis predicatorum [erasit: conventus Mediolani, in secunda] die mensis octobris

Explicit tertius liber De anima secundum lecturam parisiensem, scripte [erasit: a me, fratre Francischo de Rippa, ordinis] predicatorum [erasit: conventus] Mediolani, provincie Lombardie ??] studente ibi ?? decima mensis octobris
msItem.note: Transmitted anonymously, but the colophon at f. 251ra specifies its Parisian origin.

origin.p: Composite manuscript, copied in the Dominican convents of Pavia and Milan, as evident from the colophons of the friars Petrus Iohannes Balbus (f.25ra) and Franciscus de Rippa (f.238va and f.251ra). These colophons are difficult to read because some important information about the scribes and the location of the copy has been erased.

The Dominican provenance of the codex is confirmed by the illumination at the beginning of the second treatise of the commentary on Petrus Hispanus (f. 2r), where St. Dominic is portrayed with a black mantle and a white tunic.
Most of the texts in this manuscript are of Parisian origin.
For the Italian didactic context of the confection of the volume, see f.1v.

The possession mark, on a piece of parchment from the original lost guard, states that the part of the volume containing the logical commentaries belonged to Petrus Iohannes Balbus, who copied it in 1458: Hoc commentum super artem veterem et philosofiam est mei, fratris Petri Iohannis Balbi Ianuensis ordinis Predicatorum, quod scripsi in diversis temporibus et ipsum finivi anno Domini M°CCCCLVIII° die Va iulii. Et omnia que in ipso continentur fuerunt extracta a libello valde incorrepto. On other old fragments, now pasted on the inner front cover, we read: Iohannis Balbi. Commentum supra philosophiam Aristotelis; Commentum supra philosophiam Iohannis Balbi fratris ordinis predicatorum. [a. m.] 1458.
The watermark, a flower, is similar to Piccard XII, II 967 (Pavia, 1448).

listBibl.bibl: Kaeppeli I, p. 395–397, n° 1122 (Franciscus de Prato).
Kaeppeli III, p. 302, n° 3464 (Richaldus de Bovilla).
Meirinhos, Bibliotheca, pp. 399–400, n° 680 (where Versoris’s CsPH is qualified as “Super Praedicabilia Aristotelis” and Versoris’s QdA as “Lectiones in Perihermeneias”).

F. Amerini, “Codex e la filosofia medievale in Toscana: dal tempo di Dante alla fine del Trecento”, Codex. Studies Journal of the Società Internazionale per lo Studio del Medioevo Latino 7 (2018), pp. 3–32, at pp. 18–19, 25 (where, following Meirinhos, Versoris’s CsPH is qualified as “Super Praedicabilia Aristotelis” and Versoris’s QdA as “Lectiones in Perihermeneias”).

msIdentifier.repository: Bibliothèque d'Agglomération du Pays de Saint-Omer (Bibliothèque municipale)

msItem.title: QsP
msItem.colophon: Et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto primo Phisicorum, scripto in vico Straminis sub venerabili ac discreto my conjecture, the manuscript has a reading similar to “transcripto” viro, magistro Clemente Parmentarii, venerabilis nationis Normanne, per me Philippum Goupil, eiusdem nationis, dyocesis Rothomagentis, anno Domini M°CCCC°XLmo III°, duodecima mensis novembris. Philip Goupil

Explicit secundus Phisicorum, perscriptus in vigilia conceptionis beate Marie virginis, festis Normanne sub venerabili magistro, magistro Clemente Parmentarii per scriptorem supra dictum venerabilis Normanne nationis

Et hoc de toto libro tertio Phisicorum, scripto in vico Straminis sub venerabili magistro, magistro Clemente Parmentarii, actu regente Parisiis, per me Philippum Goupil, venerabilis Normanne nationis, anno Domini M°CCCC°XLmo III° [sic pro IIII°], die penultima mensis decembris. Philip Goupil

Et hoc de toto quarto Phisicorum, scripto in vico Straminis sub magistro Clemente Parmentarii nationis Normanne, actu regente Parisiis, per me Philippum Goupil eiusdem venerabilis nationis, anno Domini M°CCCC°XLmo III° [sic pro IIII°], die quarta mensis februarii. Philip Goupil

Et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto quinto Phisicorum, peracto in vico Straminis sub venerabili viro, magistro Clemente Parmentarii, venerabilis Normanne nationis, per me Philippum Goupil eiusdem nationis, dyocesis Rothomagensis, decima scripta die februarii, anno XLmo III° [sic pro IIII°]. P. Goupil

Et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto sexto Phisicorum Aristotelis, perscripto in die sancti Thome de Aquino sub magistro Clemente Parmentarii, per me Philippum Goupil Normanne nationis, anno Domini 1443° [sic pro 1444°]. P. Goupil
et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto septimo Phisicorum, scripto sub magistro predicto per manum predicti studentis, anno supradicto. Explicit septimus Phisicorum. P. Goupil

et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto octavo libro Phisicorum qui dicitur 'de physico auditu', scripto ut parto in vico Straminis Parisius, sub venerabili viro, magistro Clemente Permentarii, venerabilis nationis Normanne, per me, Philippum Goupil eiusdem nationis, Rothomagentis dyocesis, anno Domini 1443° [sic pro 1444°], die mercuri octava aprilis ante festum Pasche. P. Goupil

msItem.title: QdC
msItem.colophon: et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto primo Celi et mundi, scripto sub magistro Clemente Permentarii, commorante in domo reverendissimi magistri Petri Richeri in theologia licentiati, venerabilis nationis Normanne, per me, Philippum Goupil eiusdem nationis, Rothomagentis dyocesis, anno Domini 1444to, decima octava die mensis aprilis post Pascham. P. Goupil
et hoc de questione, et de toto libro quarto Celi et mundi, perscripto quindecima die mensis julii in vico Straminis sub predicto magistro per manum predicti scriptoris. P. Goupil

msItem.title: QdG
msItem.colophon: et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto primo libro De generatione et corruptione, scripto in vico Straminis in vigilia beati Bernardi, sub magistro Clementi Parmentarii, per me, Philippum Goupil, venerabilis nationis Normane, dyocesis Rothomagentis. P. Goupil
et hoc de questione, et per consequens de toto secundo libro De generatione et corruptione, scripto secunda die mensis septembris. Explicit secundus De generatione et corruptione. P. Goupil

msItem.title: QsMete
msItem.colophon: et hoc de questione, et de toto primo libro Metheororum, scripto in vico Straminis sub magistro Clemente Parmentarii venerabilis Normanne nationis, per me, Philippum Goupil, eiusdem nationis, dyocesis Rothomagensis. P. Goupil
et hoc de questione, et de toto secundo Metheororum libro, scripto in festo sancti Dominici, anno supradicto, sub magistro supradicto, per manum predicti studentis. P. Goupil

msItem.title: QdA
msItem.colophon: P. Goupil
P. Goupil
P. Goupil

msItem.title: QsSen
msItem.colophon: et hoc de questione et per consequens de toto libro De sensu et sensato, scripto in camera, per me, Philippum Goupil venerabilis nationis Normnane propter cessationes factas in universitate ab omni actu scolastico, tam occulto quam manifesto, ob occasione aliquarum iniuriarim in personam domini rectoris huius alme universitatis Parisiensis, nec non aliorum magistrorum quam plurimorum eiusdem universitatis imperperatarum, anno Domini M°CCCC°XLIIIIto septima die septembris, in vigilia conceptionis beatissime Virginis, P. Goupil

msItem.title: QsLon
msItem.colophon: et hoc de questione et de toto libro De longitudine et brevitate vite, et per consequens de tota philosophia naturali, finita anno Domini M°CCCC°XLIIII°, undecima die mensis septembris, P. Goupil

origin.p: The date is attested by Philip Goupil in the numerous colophons, most of which bear precise date indication

The manuscript was copied by Philippe Goupil, a student in the house of Pierre Richier (Petrus Richeri), under the supervision of the master Clément Parmentier (Clemens Parmentarii), as indicated by the numerous colophons throughout the codex.

provenance.p: Two possession marks attest that the manuscript was owned by Iacob Pol, a student in Paris and later a friar at the Abbey of Saint-Bertin: Ista philosophia pertinet fratri Jacobi Pol, religioso ecclesiae sancti Bertini in Sancto Audomaro, Morinensis diocesis;Hunc librum acquisivit frater Jacobus Pol, tempore quo fuit scolaris Parisiensis

bibl.note: Suprisingly not in Lohr, Aristotelica gallica

listBibl.bibl: Livesey, Science in the Monastery. Texts, Manuscripts and Learning at Saint-Bertin, Turnhout 2020, 189-192 The texts are here presented as Questiones naturales of Aristotle and ascribed to Clémens Parmentier

msIdentifier.repository: Bibliothèque d'Agglomération du Pays de Saint-Omer (Bibliothèque municipale)

msItem.title: QsP
msItem.note: Most of the QsP – particularly up to book III – display strong similarities with the questions ascribed to Versoris. However, with rare exceptions, most of the questions present a shortened text compared to the other copies. This process of simplification implies the elimination of the doubts (dubitationes) at the end of the questions, the reduction of the number of the conclusions, often reduced to the sole conclusio principalis, and the simplification of their structure, often limited to the enunciation of the main conclusion.

msItem.title: QdC
msItem.note: In the QdC the replies to the questions (including the notabilia, the conclusiones and the dubitationes) are missing. The arguments pro and contra are followed by the replies to the arguments.

msItem.title: QdG
msItem.note: The QdG represent a kind of a draft or repertoires of arguments, which mostly corresponds to the analogous arguments ascribed to Versoris. The question titles are followed by a series of arguments and their solutions. Question I.20, utrum elementa maneant formaliter in mixto, is the only one whose text is complete: it corresponds to the analogue question ascribed to Versoris.

msItem.title: QsMete
msItem.note: Only sporadically elements of the answer (notabilia) can be found. Differently from the QdG, the arguments are enounced altogether one after the other, as it is often the case in question commentaries, and the solutions are also grouped under the rubric "ad rationes". The arguments usually correspond to the ones found in the commentary ascribed to Versoris.

msItem.title: QdA
msItem.note: Most of the QdA are longer, as they usually (but not always) contain, beside the arguments and their solutions, the answer to the question, including some notabilia and conclusions (but not dubitationes). The text is different from the questions ascribed to Versoris.

msItem.title: QsLon
msItem.note: The questions on the Parva naturalia are again a repertoire of arguments and replies (presented in separate blocks). The arguments corresponds to those in the commentary ascribed to Versoris, but they are adapted in the new structure. For instance, in the first question, Utrum corpus animatum sit subiectum in libris parvorum naturalium, the reply to the first argument contains, in the commentary ascribed to Versoris, a reference to what has been said in the body of the question (“patet solutio ex dictis”). Since this part of the text is missing in the Saint-Omer manuscript, its content is summarized in the reply to the arguments, which, as a consequence, is much longer than in the commentary ascribed to Versoris.

origin.p: Dating attested in the final colophon: Et sic est finis libri De longitudine et brevitate vite, et per consequens de tota philosophia naturali Aristotelis, finita per me, fratrem Georgium de Brabantia, religiosum sancti Bertini, actu studentem Parisius in facultate atrium, anno Domini millesimo CCCC° LXX°, mensis Ianuarii, altera die festivitatis beati Pauli apostoli, hora fere prima post prandium. Deo laus. Tu qui suxisti de Virgine virgineum lac, regni celestis scriptorem participem fac, f. 115ra

The manuscript was copied by the benedictine monk Georgius de Brabantia, student at Paris Arts Faculty, as evident from the several signatures at the end of the texts (for instance, f. 11va, Frater Georgius de Brabantia; f. 56vb Georgius; f. 72vb, Georgius de Brabantia), colophons of the QsP the QdA (Et sic est finis tertii De anima, finiti per me, fratrem Georgium de Brabantia, actu studentem Parisius in facultate artium. Frater Georgius de Brabantia, f. 109va), the QdSom (Georgius de Brabantia, f. 114rb), the final colophon (transcribed below), as well as from the initium of the QdSom (Frater Georgius de Brabantia, f. 113ra). The name of Georgius de Brabantia also appears in the philaters of the illustrations found in several places of the manuscript, particularly at the beginning of the books. QsP, book II, f. 18ra: Et sic est finis secundi Phisicorum Aristotelis, finitus per manum Georgii Brabantie. Frater Georgius de Brabantia
At the end of book I QsP, definitions (Incipiunt diffinitiones et auctoritates primi Phisicorum)

provenance.p: The codex belonged to Carolus de Miraumont (1566-1637), a Benedictine monk at Saint-Bertin, whose name appears several times on f. 112rb et on f. 115rb

listBibl.bibl: Gil, Saint-Omer gothique. Les arts figuratifs à Saint-Omer à la fin du Moyen Age 1250-1550: peinture, vitrail, sculpture, arts du livre, Valenciennes 2004, 379
This manuscript is not mentioned in Charles Lohr's Aristotelica Gallica II, which skips directly from manuscript nr. 596 to 598 (p. 100-101)

summary.ab: None; only the Parisian origin of the texts is made explicit.
Versoris's texts are bound after Eusebius of Caesarea's De evangelica praeparatione, printed in 1473 in Venice by Leonhardus Aurl (Hain 6700*; GW 9442).

msItem.title: QdA
msItem.colophon: Ex hec de tertio libro etc. 1471, in universitate Lipsiensi sunt pronuntiata et Parisius collecta

msItem.title: QdLong
msItem.colophon: Petrus Hawindorn, persecutor rusticorum, 1472. [alia manu:] Hunc librum continentem tractatus De anima, De celo et mundo, De generatione et corruptione, De methauris, De sensu et sensato, De sompno et vigilia, dedit in refrigerium anime sue honorabilis dominus Georgius Ichenstarfer, artium baccalarius, capellanus civium Ligsalz in Monaco conventui fratrum heremitarum sancti Augustini, ibidem anno 1476, in profesto Conversionis sancti Pauli

origin.p: The texts, all written by Petrus Hawindorn, resulted from a pronuntiatio at Leipzig University, as stated in the colophon of QdA (f. 211r) and in the final colophon (f. 320r). The manuscript was later owned by the bachelor Georgius Ichenstarfer, who donated it to the convent of the Augustinian Hermits in 1476, as stated after the final colophon (f. 320r).

provenance.p: Donated by Georgius Ichenstarfer to the convent of the Augustinian Hermits in 1476.

listBibl.bibl: G. Scherer, Verzeichniss der Manuscripte und Inkunabeln der Vadianischen Bibliothek in St. Gallen, St. Gallen 1864, 137–138, 214.
Scarpatetti, Katalog, III, p. 21, no. 55; p. 306, fig. 387.

summary.ab: No. The texts are transmitted anonymously, but the parchment title shield affixed to the back cover clearly indicates their Parisian origin.

msItem.title: QsPri
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto libro primo et secundo Priorum Aristotelis, finito Parisius per manus Clementis Bohemi de Melnyk, anno Domini M°CCCC°XLIX°, in anno Iubileo

msItem.title: QsPos
msItem.colophon: Finitus liber Posteriorum per manus Clementis de Melnyk de Bohemia, Parisius, in domo magistri Alberti Scriptoris

msItem.title: QsSE
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de tota logica vetere et nova, per manus Clementis de Melnyk Bohemi

origin.p: The manuscript was copied in Paris, in the school of the master Albertus Scriptoris, by the Czech student Clemens de Melnyk, as attested by the colophons: f. 127vb; f. 156rb; f. 196rb.

note.p: Praha, NK, X H 21, which refers to a manuscript of logical content copied by a Bohemian student in the school of the Parisian master Albertus Scriptoris and subsequently brought to Prague.

Berlin, Staatsbibliothek – PK, lat. fol. 402, which features a watermark identical to one found in this manuscript (a unicorn, Piccard X 3 2010, particularly visible on fol. 21), widespread in the years 1448–1449.

provenance.p: The manuscript belonged to the master Iohannes Guldin, as attested by the parchment title shield affixed to the back cover: Disputata veteris et nove loyce parisiensis. A magistro Iohanne Guldin. Guldin, a jurist, served as a canon in Isny in 1462/1463 and died in Constance in 1469 or 1470. From Guldin’s library, the codex passed into the possession of the library of Constance Cathedral, as evidenced by the binding – typical of manuscripts from Constance – and by fragments attached to the cover that mention individuals from the city. From Constance, the manuscript made its way to Weingarten. On f. 3r, marg. sup., one reads: Monasterii Weingartensis, anno 1630.

listBibl.bibl: F. M. Bartoš, Husitika a bohemika několika knihoven německých a švýcarských [Hussitica and Bohemica in some German and Swiss libraries], Praha 1932, 40.

M. S. Buhl, Die Handschriften der Württembergischen Landesbibliothek Stuttgart. Bd. 4.1. Codices philologici (HB VIII 1 –31). Codices Arabici (HB IX 1 –2). Codices philosophici (HB X 1 –30), Wiesbaden 1972, 60–61.

msItem.title: QdA
msItem.note: This is the only text contained in the manuscript.
The commentary includes abundant marginal notes up to f. 41v.

origin.origDate: 1490–1491, as inferred from the watermarks.
origin.p: The watermarks, typical of the Upper Rhine and Schwarzwald regions, contain the coat of arms of the city of Offenburg (Piccard: Turm XVII 191/193).

provenance.p: The manuscript arrived in Stuttgart in 1765.

msItem.title: CsY
msItem.note: This text is part of a series of anonymous notes, schemata, and short questions on Aristotle’s natural books (Physics; De celo, two commentaries; De generatione et corruptione; Meteorologica; De anima; De sensu et sensato; De memoria et reminiscentia) and moral works (Ethics, two commentaries; Politics). The commentary on the Politics that immediately follows (ff. 120r–136v) is copied by the same hand and begins with an incipit that makes a smooth transition from the CsY (Expeditis duabus partibus moralis philosophie, restat dicere de parte tertia). It shares the same structure: the text is divided into chapters followed by sections of doubts (dubia). As in CsY, chapters are introduced with formulas such as: Iste est liber secundus, in quod Philosophus determinat… and the dubia sections with phrases like: Ad evidentiam autem meliorem huius erunt duo dubia.

origin.p: No explicit information about the provenance or dating of this manuscript can be found in the texts. However, the binding—produced in Danzig between 1433 and 1436—suggests the manuscript was copied before that date. The watermarks indicate a likely origin between the late 1420s and early 1430s, in a region between South Tyrol and Bavaria. For instance, the oxhead with flower at f. 1 is quite similar (though not identical) to Piccard II.3 XII 65 (1424, 1426, München); the oxhead with cross at ff. 11, 69 resembles Piccard II.3 XI 117/118 (1430–1436, Dillingen, Tramin, among others); the oxhead with flower at f. 145 corresponds to Piccard II.3 XIII 291 (1422–1424, Innsbruck, Landshut, Mergentheim, Nürnberg, Schwäbisch Hall); and the oxhead with flower at ff. 347, 355, 360 is quite similar to Piccard II.3 XII 204/205 (1434–1436, Freising, Haselbach, Kamerau, Marienburg) and very similar to Briquet 14782 (Tyrol, 1432; Brunswick, 1435). I was not able to identify the oxhead with nostrils (f. 137) or the crown (f. 267) in either Briquet or Piccard.

provenance.p: The manuscript was later located in Wiblingen (near Ulm), as evidenced by its mention in the 1632 catalogue. On f. 2r is written: Ex libris Monasterii Wiblingen. In 1822, the manuscript entered the possession of the Tübinger Wilhelmsstift and was acquired by the Royal Library in 1885.

origin.p: The part copied by Iohannes Nickel dates to 1465, as stated in the colophon of a set of anonymous QdA: Finis huius anno Domini M°CCCCLXV in profesto Mathei apostoli et ewangeliste [add. Petrus Nickel Lipsius per Iohannem Nickel], f. 90r.

The texts contained in this codex were copied by Iohannes Nickel from Oldernheim, enrolled at Leipzig in 1461 (ff. 4r–91v, 96r–173v, 178v, 179r–292v, 296r–387r), and by his relative Petrus Nickel (alias Schnorrenpeyl), scholar and clergyman in Heidelberg, who transcribed texts in this manuscript from 1525 onwards (ff. 1r–2v, 93r–95v, 174r–177v, 178v, 389r–394v). Versoris’s commentaries were copied by Iohannes Nickel and travelled from Leipzig to Heidelberg.

listBibl.bibl: Walz, Die historischen und philosophischen Handschriften, 210-215.not in Lohr.

summary.ab: On the first leaf, a medieval hand noted: “metaphysica Versoris”.

origin.p: 1490, as indicated by the colophon in the hand of the copyist: Finis huius anno Domini millesimo quadragentesimo nonagesimo.

provenance.p: The codex belonged to Andrzej Frycz Modrzewski, who held the hereditary title of mayor (advocatus) of Wolbórz. On the inner back cover, we read: Sum Andreae Advocatilis Volboriensis, de Trzepnicza oriundi, arcium baccalarij. Since Andrzej Modrzewski studied at the University of Krakow, it is very probable that the codex originated there.

listBibl.bibl: J. Kaliszuk, S. Szyller, Inwentarze rękopisów biblioteki narodowej. Inwentarz rękopisów do połowy XVI wieku w zbiorach Biblioteki Narodowej, Warszawa 2012, 51.

summary.ab: The text is ascribed to Versor in the quoted colophon.

msItem.title: Qdente
msItem.colophon: Explicit commentum sive questiones Versoris, magistri Parisiensis, super tractatum De ente et essentia sancti Thome de Aquino doctoris, scriptum in studio Magdeburgensi per fratrem Johannem Fleckel ordinis predicatorum, nativum Wiennensem, provincie Theotonie, finitum feria quarta post Letare, anno Domini 1462.

origin.p: Most of the texts contained in the manuscript, including numerous tracts by Thomas Aquinas, were copied in the Dominican studium in Magdeburg by the friar Johannes Fleckel, originally from Vienna, as is evident from the colophon of Versor’s commentary.

listBibl.bibl: F. Czeike, Verzeichnis der Handschriften des Dominikanerkonventes in Wien bis zum Ende des 16. Jahrhunderts [typescript], Vienna, 1952, 77–89.
C. Vansteenkiste, “Codici tomistici della biblioteca Domenicana di Vienna (Austria)”, Angelicum 38 (1961), 133–165, at 157.
I. W. Frank, Hausstudium und Universitätsstudium der Wiener Dominikaner bis 1500, Vienna, 1968, 49, 149 n. 90, 168 n. 39, 196, 209, 216, 244–245, 250.
F. Unterkircher, H. Horninger, F. Lackner, Die datierten Handschriften in Wien außerhalb der Österreichischen Nationalbibliothek bis zum Jahre 1600. 1: Text. 2: Plates, Vienna, 1981, no. 61.

summary.ab: None, only the Parisian origin of the texts is specified.

msItem.title: QsPor
msItem.colophon: Expliciunt sententiae super libro universalium Porphyrii, finite in die passionis beatissime Ludmille, vidue, patrone et domine incliti regni Bohemie, in collegio Reczkonis, ductive perutiliter in notitiam universalium ipsorum quidditates et essentias lucidissime demonstrantes

msItem.title: QsPeri
msItem.colophon: Terminantur sententie totius veteris artis in alma universitate studii Parisiensis questionatim collecte per Wenceslaum de Wrben, baccalarium artium, ad universitatem Pragensem inde allate, magnam vim et intelligentiam textuum Philosophi in se lucide continentes. Scripte Prage in collegio Sanctissime Marie virginis, anno Domini 1450 quarto ydus Octobris

msItem.title: QsApri
msItem.colophon: Et hec de questione, et per consequens de toto libro Priorum, in collegio sanctissime Marie virginis Nationis Bohemorum, in domo Reczek, anno etc. liii, ante festum Wenceslai, tempore quo terminator presentis libri examen baccalariatus intravit gradumque sub reverendo magistro Stanislao de Welwar terminavit. Eodem anno sub decursu pauci temporis rex Ladislaus Pragam ingrediens in festo sanctorum Symonis et Iude in Castro Pragensi laudabiliter est coronatus

msItem.title: QsApos
msItem.colophon: Et sic est finis glose Parisiensis super utroque libro Posteriorum, feria quinta ante Purificationem, anno Domini 1454

msItem.title: QsSE
msItem.colophon: Et in hoc terminantur Parisiensia puncta / in se continentia superficialiter cuncta / que Aristoteles in suis libris oculte tradidit / eosque ad legendum et non intelligendum edidit. Compositorque horum / verus zelator discipulorum, animum suum preparavit / pro suoque posse hanc manifestavit [rubeo: Terminantur puncta parisiensia super nova et veteri logica, comparata per Martinum de Glathovia anno etc. LII]

origin.p: The texts were copied at Prague University, at the Rezeck College, by different scribes, probably from an exemplar brought from Paris by Wenceslaus de Wrben, as indicated by the colophon (f. 101va). One of the scribes notes that he passed the bachelor examination under Master Stanislaus de Velvary. The final part of the codex, which is undated, was acquired by Martinus de Glatovia. The codex arrived at the Dominican convent in Vienna between the end of the 15th century and the beginning of the 16th century.
Colophon of QsPor, f. 34rb; colophon of the Questions on the ars vetus, f. 101va; colophon of QsApri, f. 125vb; colophon of QsApos, f. 164rb; colophon of the Questions on the ars nova, f. 210vb.

note.p: Praha, NK, V G 22, f. 266r and Schlägl, Prämonstratenserstift, 116 Cpl. f. 187r, which contain the same colophon of f. 210vb.
Praha, NK, IV F 8, which was copied by Martinus de Glatovia.
Praha, KMK, M 40/2, Praha, KMK, M 81 and Praha, NK, X H 21, in which Stanislaus de Velvary is mentioned.

Lawrence (KA), Kenneth Spencer Library, MS C 210 and Vodňany, Městské muzeuma galerie Vodňany, G 11 R, also copied in Prague around the same time, in which the texts are presented as "Parisian glosses".

provenance.p: The codex arrived at the Dominican convent in Vienna between the end of the 15th century and the beginning of the 16th century.

listBibl.bibl: F. Unterkircher, H. Horninger, F. Lackner, Die datierten Handschriften in Wien außerhalb der Österreichischen Nationalbibliothek bis zum Jahre 1600, Wien 1981, 61–62, Abb. 229, 240, 243, 250.
Green-Pedersen, The Tradition of the Topics in the Middle Ages, 401.

summary.ab: ff. 129r-133r are filled with schematical notes on the subject matter of the commentary.

origin.origDate: Versoris's commentary is undated, but on the basis of the dates of the texts copied in the same manuscript and the scribe's university cursus, it can probably be situated between 1444, when he became a bachelor, and 1451, when he obtained his master's degree. Since Donatus copied Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics in 1450, it is very likely that he transcribed Versoris's commentary on this text around the same time.
origin.p: The manuscript was copied in Leipzig and Chemnitz by Donatus Kluge de Rosswein, as attested by the following colophons: Et in hoc terminatur textus Ethice Aristotelis, completus per me, Donatum Cluge de Rewsweyn, in Lipczk, anno Domini 1450, feria quarta post festum Viti, f. 292v; Explicit Boethius, De disciplina scolarium, finitus a me, Donato Cluge, in Kemnicz, anno Domini M CCCC XLVII, f. 484r; Et hec de tertio libro De anima Aristotelis, et per consequens de toto dicta sufficiant, completa scriptaque per me, Donatum, in alma universitate Lypczensi, anno Domini 1440, f. 521r.

listBibl.bibl: S. Kądzielski, W. Mrozowicz, Catalogus codicum Medii Aevi manuscriptorum qui in bibliotheca Universitatis Wratislaviensis asservantur. Signa 6055–6124 comprehendens (Codices Milichiani, vol. 1), Wratislaviae, 1998, p. 223–226.

summary.ab: The QsE are attributed to Versoris by a hand different from that of the scribe, which, after “et sic est finis”, added: “questionum Iohannis Versoris super Ethicorum Aristotelis”. The same hand, probably that of Paulus Schwoffheim, also wrote the list of questions on ff. 193rb–194vb.

msItem.title: QsPol
msItem.author: Versoris
msItem.colophon: Et tantum de isto octavo, et per consequens de toto libro Politicorum Aristotelis, 1468

origin.p: 1468, as attested by the colophon of the QsPol, f. 341v. A table of contents was added in 1497, probably by Schwoffheim; it follows immediately after the text on ff. 341vb–342va.

history.provenance: The manuscript belonged to the Leipzig master Paulus Schwoffheim de Görlitz, who inherited it from his uncle, the master Andreas Rüdiger de Görlitz, as recorded on the inner front cover: Liber magistri Pauli Suoffheym de Gorlicz, quem obtinui ex doctore Andrea Rudiger de Gorlicz, gelicis memorie.
